pages.title: 1. An auxiliary lemma on varieties with operators

blocks.html: 1. An auxiliary lemma on varieties with operators

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme acting on the left on an S-prescheme V, W a closed sub-S-prescheme of V, and N its stabilizer in G, the subgroup of G whose points with values in an S′ over S are the \(g \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}')\) such that \(g \cdot \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{S}'} = \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{S}'}\). We equip \((\mathbf{Sch}_{/\mathrm{S}})\) with the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology, and identify G, V, M with the corresponding sheaves (cf. IV). We shall therefore be reasoning in the category of sheaves on \((\mathbf{Sch})_{/\mathrm{S}}\), and in this section the word "locally" refers to the topology on \((\mathbf{Sch})_{/\mathrm{S}}\) that we have just specified. Note that N is a sheaf; consider the quotient sheaf G/N. One sees at once that it is isomorphic to the following functor: to every S′ over S, one associates the set of subsheaves W′ of \(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) that are locally conjugate to \(\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) under the group G. Let X be the subsheaf of \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{V}\) whose value, for every S′ over S, is the set of pairs \((\mathrm{W}', v)\), where W′ is as above and \(v\) is a section of W′ over S′ (hence a section of \(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) over S′). Let Z be the inverse image of X in \(\mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{V}\), so that we have the cartesian diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Z} \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathrm{X} \ar[d]^-{i} \\ \mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{V} \ar[r] & \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{V}, } \] where i is the canonical immersion, and the second horizontal arrow comes from the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\). Since the latter assigns to the point \(g \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}')\) the subsheaf \(g \cdot \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) of \(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), one sees that \(\mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{S}')\) is the set of pairs \((g,v) \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}') \times \mathrm{V}(\mathrm{S}')\) such that \(v \in g \cdot \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{S}'}(\mathrm{S}')\). Consequently, Z is isomorphic to the sheaf \(\mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{W}\), via the isomorphism \[ \mathrm{G} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{W} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Z} \] defined by \((g,w) \mapsto (g, g\cdot w)\). Thus the preceding cartesian diagram yields the cartesian diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{W} \ar[r]^-{q} \ar[d]_-{\lambda} & \mathrm{X} \ar[d]^-{i} \\ \mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{V} \ar[r] & \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{V} } \] where \(\lambda(g,w) = (g, g\cdot w)\), hence \(q(g,w) = (\overline{g}, g\cdot w)\), where \(\overline{g}\) denotes the image of \(g\) under the canonical map \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}') \to (\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N})(\mathrm{S}')\). Finally, one sees from the preceding diagram that \(\mathrm{Z} \to \mathrm{X}\) makes Z a principal bundle with base X and group N acting on the right by \((g,v)\cdot n = (gn, v)\), so that in the second diagram, \(q : \mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{W} \to \mathrm{X}\) makes \(\mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{W}\) a principal bundle with base X and group N acting on the right by \[ (g,w)\cdot n = (gn, n^{-1}\cdot w). \]

We summarize the main morphisms above in the following diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ & \mathrm{G}\times_{\mathrm{S}}\mathrm{W} \ar[d]^-{q} \ar[dr]^-{\varphi} & \\ \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N} & \mathrm{X} \ar[l]_-{p} \ar[r]^-{\psi} \ar[d]^-{i} & \mathrm{V} \\ & \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\times_{\mathrm{S}}\mathrm{V} \ar@{-->}[ul]^-{\mathrm{pr}_1} \ar@{-->}[ur]_-{\mathrm{pr}_2} & } \] where \(\psi = \mathrm{pr}_2 \circ i\) and \(\varphi = \psi \circ q\), i.e. \(\varphi(g,w) = g\cdot w\). If \(v\) is a section of V over S, the subsheaf \(\mathrm{X}_v\) of X that is the inverse image of this section under \(\psi\) is given by \(\mathrm{X}_v(\mathrm{S}') =\) the set of subsheaves W′ of \(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) that are locally conjugate to \(\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) under G and contain the section \(v_{\mathrm{S}'}\) of \(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), while the subsheaf of \(\mathrm{G}\times_{\mathrm{S}}\mathrm{W}\) that is the inverse image of \(v\) under \(\varphi\) is isomorphic to the subsheaf \(\mathrm{M}_v\) of G given by \(\mathrm{M}_v(\mathrm{S}') =\) the set of \(g \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}')\) such that \(v_{\mathrm{S}'} \in g\cdot \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}')\), i.e. such that \(g^{-1}v_{\mathrm{S}'} \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}')\). If \(v\) is a section of W and not merely of V, then \(\mathrm{M}_v\) obviously contains N.

In these explicit descriptions, we have not used the fact that G, V, W are representable (nor that the site on which we are working is defined in terms of preschemes!). But suppose now that N is representable and faithfully flat and quasi-compact over S, and that G/N is representable. When S is the spectrum of a field, and G is of finite type over \(k\), it is known that this hypothesis is automatically satisfied (VIII 6 and VIB 11.18). One then sees from the preceding cartesian diagram, using the theory of faithfully flat quasi-compact descent and the fact that \(\mathrm{Z} \to \mathrm{G}\times_{\mathrm{S}}\mathrm{V}\) is a closed immersion, that X is representable (it is obtained by descent of the closed subprescheme Z of \(\mathrm{G}\times_{\mathrm{S}}\mathrm{V}\) along the faithfully flat quasi-compact morphism \(\mathrm{G}\times_{\mathrm{S}}\mathrm{V} \to \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\times_{\mathrm{S}}\mathrm{V}\)). Hence the diagram (D) is a diagram of morphisms of preschemes over S.

We assume from now on that S is the spectrum of a field \(k\), and that G, V, W are of finite type over \(k\). Let \(\mathfrak{n}\) be the Lie algebra of N; we then have \(\dim \mathrm{N} \leqslant \mathrm{rang}\,\mathfrak{n}\), with equality if and only if N is smooth over \(k\) (Exp VI1). Let \(a \in \mathrm{W}(k)\), and consider the subscheme \(\mathrm{M}_a\) of G defined above, containing N and isomorphic to \(\varphi^{-1}(a)\); we denote by \(\mathfrak{m}_a\) its Zariski tangent space at the identity element \(e\) of N, so that we have \[ (1) \qquad \mathfrak{n} \subset \mathfrak{m}_a, \qquad \dim \mathrm{N} \leqslant \mathrm{rang}\,\mathfrak{n} \leqslant \mathfrak{m}_a. \]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1

With the preceding notation:

a) Consider the following conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(\mathfrak{n} = \mathfrak{m}_a\) and N is smooth over \(k\).

  2. (i bis)

    \(\dim \mathrm{N} = \mathrm{rang}_k\,\mathfrak{m}_a\).

  3. (ii)

    The morphism \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{V}\) is unramified at \((\overline{e},a)\).

  4. (iii)

    \(\mathrm{M}_a\) and N coincide in a neighborhood of \(e\).

Then we have the implications (i) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (i bis) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (iii).

b) Suppose \(\varphi : \mathrm{G}\times_{\mathrm{S}}\mathrm{W} \to \mathrm{V}\) is smooth at \((e,a)\). Then \(\mathrm{M}_a\) is smooth over \(k\) at \(e\), and \(\psi\) is smooth at \((\overline{e},a)\).

Proof. a) The equivalence of (i) and (i bis) follows at once from the relations (1) and from the fact noted above that N is smooth over \(k\) if and only if \(\dim \mathrm{N} = \mathrm{rang}_k\,\mathfrak{n}\). On the other hand, consider the inclusion morphism \(\mathrm{N} \to \mathrm{M}_a\); it is well known2 that if N is smooth over \(k\) at \(e\) and the tangent map at \(e\) is surjective, then \(\mathrm{N} \to \mathrm{M}_a\) is smooth at \(e\), hence (being an immersion) is an isomorphism at \(e\), which shows that (i) implies (iii). To prove the equivalence of (ii) and (iii), consider as above \(\mathrm{X}_a = \psi^{-1}(a)\) and use the isomorphism \(\mathrm{M}_a \simeq \varphi^{-1}(a) \simeq q^{-1}(\mathrm{X}_a)\) to obtain a morphism \(p_a : \mathrm{M}_a \to \mathrm{X}_a\) that makes \(\mathrm{M}_a\) a principal homogeneous bundle with group \(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{X}_a}\). Consider the following diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{M}_a \ar[d]_-{p_a} & \mathrm{N} \ar[l]_-{j_a} \ar[d] \\ \mathrm{X}_a \ar[r]_-{j_a'} & \mathrm{S} = \mathrm{Spec}(k), } \] where \(\mathrm{Spec}(k) \to \mathrm{X}_a\) is defined by the point \((\overline{e},a)\) of X, and \(j_a : \mathrm{N} \to \mathrm{M}_a\) is the canonical immersion. To say that \(\psi\) is unramified at \((\overline{e},a)\) means that \(j_a'\) is an open immersion, or again that it induces an isomorphism \(\mathrm{S} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{X}_a,a})\). Since \(p_a\) is flat, this is equivalent to saying that the morphism deduced from the preceding one by the base change \(\mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{M}_a,e}) \to \mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{X}_a,a})\) is an isomorphism; but this deduced morphism is none other than the morphism \(\mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{N},e}) \to \mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{M}_a,e})\), which proves the equivalence of (ii) and (iii).
Note, moreover, that the proof shows that conditions (ii), (iii) imply the following condition, apparently stronger than (iii):
(iii bis) N is an open and closed subscheme of M.
b) The first assertion comes from the fact that \(\mathrm{M}_a\) is isomorphic to \(\varphi^{-1}(a)\), the second from the fact that \(q\) is flat and \(q(e,a) = (\overline{e},a)\).

pages.title: 2. Density theorem and theory of the regular points of G

blocks.html: 2. Density theorem and theory of the regular points of G

We are going to apply the constructions and notation of the preceding section in the case where G is a connected smooth algebraic group over \(k\), where \(\mathrm{V} = \mathrm{G}\), on which G acts by inner automorphisms, and where W is a connected smooth algebraic subgroup H of G. We denote by \(\mathfrak{g}\) the Lie algebra of G, by \(\mathfrak{h}\) that of H, by N the normalizer of H in G, and by \(\mathfrak{n}\) the Lie algebra of N. If \(a \in \mathrm{G}(k)\), we shall again denote, as in Section 1, by \(\mathrm{M}_a\) the inverse of its transporter into H, so that if \(a \in \mathrm{H}(k)\), we have \(\mathrm{N} \subset \mathrm{M}_a\); in this case, we denote by \(\mathfrak{m}_a\) the Zariski tangent space of \(\mathrm{M}_a\) at the identity element \(e\) of G. Note that \[ \mathfrak{h} \subset \mathfrak{n} \subset \mathfrak{m}_a \subset \mathfrak{g} \qquad \text{for } a \in \mathrm{H}(k). \] We shall need to use the following

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.0

For \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{N}^0\) to hold, it is necessary and sufficient that \((\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h})^{\mathrm{H}} = 0\) (where the left-hand side denotes the subspace of invariants under the action of H deduced from the adjoint representation). When this condition is satisfied, N is smooth and we have \(\dim \mathrm{X} = \dim \mathrm{G}\). In any case, \(\dim \mathrm{X} \leqslant \dim \mathrm{G}\), and this inequality is an equality if and only if H is of finite index in N.

Indeed, we have seen (II 5.2.3 (i)) that \(\mathfrak{n}\) is equal to the inverse image of \((\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h})^{\mathrm{H}}\) under the morphism \(\mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}\), so \((\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h})^{\mathrm{H}} = 0\) is equivalent to \(\mathfrak{h} = \mathfrak{n}\), which is also equivalent (H being a connected smooth algebraic subgroup of the algebraic group N) to \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{N}^0\) (cf. VI.2). This obviously implies that N is smooth. On the other hand, we have \[ \dim \mathrm{X} = (\dim \mathrm{G} - \dim \mathrm{N}) + \dim \mathrm{H} = \dim \mathrm{G} - (\dim \mathrm{N} - \dim \mathrm{H}), \] hence we have \[ \dim \mathrm{X} \leqslant \dim \mathrm{G}, \] equality being attained if and only if \(\dim \mathrm{H} = \dim \mathrm{N}\), i.e. if and only if H is of finite index in N. This is the case in particular if \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{N}^0\), which completes the proof of 2.0.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.1

Let G be a connected smooth algebraic group over the algebraically closed field \(k\), H a connected smooth algebraic subgroup, N its normalizer, \(\mathfrak{g}, \mathfrak{h}, \mathfrak{n}\) the Lie algebras, \(\mathrm{X} = \mathrm{G}\times^{\mathrm{N}}\mathrm{H}\) the scheme (fibered over \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\) with typical fiber H) introduced in Section 1, and \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) the canonical morphism (whose image is also the image of \(\varphi : \mathrm{G}\times\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\) defined by \(\varphi(g,h) = \mathrm{int}(g)h = ghg^{-1}\)). The following conditions are all equivalent:

  1. (i)

    H contains a Cartan subgroup (XII 1) C of G.

  2. (i bis)

    H has the same reductive rank and the same nilpotent rank (XII 1) as G.

  3. (ii)

    H contains a maximal torus T of G, and \((\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h})^{\mathrm{T}} = 0\).

  4. (iii)

    The set of conjugates of H containing a given maximal torus is finite and non-empty, and H is of finite index in its normalizer.

  5. (iv)

    There exists \(a \in \mathrm{H}(k)\) which is contained in only finitely many conjugates of H (or merely such that \(\psi^{-1}(a)\) has an isolated point), and H is of finite index in its normalizer.

  6. (iv bis)

    The morphism \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) is generically quasi-finite (i.e. there exists a dense open subset of X on which \(\psi\) is quasi-finite), and H is of finite index in its normalizer.

  7. (v)

    There exists a dense open subset U in G such that for every \(x \in \mathrm{U}(k)\), the set of conjugates of H containing \(x\) is finite and non-empty, i.e. \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) is dominant and generically quasi-finite.

  8. (vi)

    There exists a dense open subset U of G such that every \(x \in \mathrm{U}(k)\) is contained in a conjugate of H, i.e. \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) is dominant.

  9. (vii)

    There exists \(a \in \mathrm{H}(k)\) such that the subspace of \(\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}\) of fixed points of \(\mathrm{ad}_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}}(a)\) is zero.

Moreover, these conditions imply that H is its own connected normalizer, i.e. N is smooth and \(\dim \mathrm{H} = \dim \mathrm{N}\), and that \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) is generically étale.

Proof. By 2.0, we have \(\dim \mathrm{X} \leqslant \dim \mathrm{G}\), with equality if and only if \(\dim \mathrm{H} = \dim \mathrm{N}\), i.e. H of finite index in N. From the inequality \(\dim \mathrm{X} \leqslant \dim \mathrm{G}\) it follows that \(\psi\) is dominant if and only if it is dominant and generically quasi-finite, or again if and only if \(\psi\) is generically quasi-finite and \(\dim \mathrm{X} = \dim \mathrm{G}\). Since this last equality also means, by 2.0, that H is of finite index in N, we have proved the equivalence of (vi), (v), (iv bis). The equivalence of (iv) and (iv bis) is immediate.

The equivalence of (i) and (i bis) is immediate from the definitions, and left to the reader. On the other hand, if H contains a Cartan subgroup C of G, it contains the maximal torus T of C, which is a maximal torus of G. Since C is the centralizer of T, its Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{c}\) is given by \[ \mathfrak{c} = \mathfrak{g}^{\mathrm{T}} \] (II, 5.2.3 (ii)). Hence, since \(\mathrm{H} \supset \mathrm{C}\), whence \(\mathfrak{h} \supset \mathfrak{c}\), it follows that \(\mathfrak{h} \subset \mathfrak{g}^{\mathrm{T}}\), which by I, 4.7.3 is equivalent to the relation \[ (x) \qquad (\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h})^{\mathrm{T}} = 0. \] Conversely, suppose that H contains the maximal torus T and that the preceding relation holds, i.e. that we have \(\mathfrak{h} \supset \mathfrak{n}\); I claim that H contains the centralizer C of T (which will establish (i) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (ii)). This follows from the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1.1

Let G be a smooth algebraic group over the field \(k\), T a subgroup of multiplicative type of G, C its connected centralizer (equal to the centralizer of T if G is connected and T a torus, (XII 6.6 b))), and H a smooth subgroup of G containing T. For H to contain C, it is necessary and sufficient that its Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{h}\) contain that, \(\mathfrak{c}\), of C.

Indeed, it is known (XI 2.4) that \(\mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})\) is smooth over \(k\), so C is smooth over \(k\); likewise \(\mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm{H}}(\mathrm{T})\) is smooth over \(k\). Now \(\mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm{H}}(\mathrm{T}) = \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}) \cap \mathrm{H}\) has \(\mathfrak{h}\cap\mathfrak{c}\) as Lie algebra, so the hypothesis implies that the smooth subgroup \(\mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm{H}}(\mathrm{T})\) of the smooth group \(\mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})\) has the same Lie algebra, hence it contains the connected component C of the latter, hence H contains C. Q.E.D.

Let us prove the equivalence of (i) and (iii), which amounts to proving that if H contains the maximal torus T of G, then the condition \(\mathrm{H} \supset \mathrm{C}\) (which is also equivalent to (x) above, as we have just seen) is equivalent to the fact that H is of finite index in its normalizer and that the set of conjugates of H containing T is finite. If H contains C, hence if we have (x), then a fortiori \[ (xx) \qquad (\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h})^{\mathrm{H}} = 0, \] now it is known that \(\mathfrak{n}\) is the inverse image of the left-hand side of the preceding relation under the canonical homomorphism \(\mathfrak{g} \to \mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}\) (II 5.2.3 (i)), so the preceding relation also means, by virtue of 2.0, that \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{N}^0\); a fortiori H is of finite index in its normalizer. Consider now the diagram of subgroups \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{T} \ar[r] & \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})\cap \mathrm{H} \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathrm{H} \ar[d] \\ & \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})\cap \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{H}) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{H}) \\ & \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T}) & } \] Using the conjugation theorem for maximal tori in H (XII 6.6 a)), one sees that every conjugate of H containing T is conjugate to H by an element of \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})(k)\), hence that the set of conjugates of H containing T is in one-to-one correspondence with the set of points of \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})/\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})\cap\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{H})\) with coefficients in \(k\); now since \(\mathrm{H} \supset \mathrm{C}\) we have \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T}) \cap \mathrm{H} \supset \mathrm{C}\), so the preceding set is a quotient of \((\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})/\mathrm{C})(k)\), which is a finite set, hence it is finite. This proves that (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (iii). Conversely, suppose (iii), i.e. \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})/\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})\cap\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{H})\) finite and \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{H})/\mathrm{H}\) finite. Using again the conjugation theorem in H, one sees further that the homomorphism \[ \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T}) \cap \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{H})/\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T}) \cap \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{H})/\mathrm{H} \] induced by the preceding diagram is bijective on points with values in \(k\) (in fact, it is an isomorphism); hence, since the latter is finite, so is the former, so \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T}) \cap \mathrm{H}\) is of finite index in \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})\), hence contains \(\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})^0\), hence \(\mathrm{H} \supset \mathrm{C}\). Thus, (i), (i bis), (ii), (iii) are equivalent conditions.

Let us prove that (ii)\(\Rightarrow\) (vii). One sees at once that conditions (ii) and (vii) are each invariant under an extension \(k \to k'\) of the base field, with \(k'\) algebraically closed, which allows us to assume that \(k\) is of infinite transcendence degree over its prime subfield. Then it is well known (and immediately verified) that there exists an element \(a\) of \(\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})\) such that the subgroup of \(\mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})\) it generates is dense in T for the Zariski topology. From this one easily concludes \((\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h})^{\mathrm{T}} = (\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h})^{\mathrm{ad}(a)}\), and since by hypothesis the left-hand side is zero, one concludes (vii).
Let us prove (vii) \(\Rightarrow\) (vi). This implication is contained in the following result, which makes 2.1 more precise:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.2

Let G be a smooth algebraic group over a field \(k\), H a smooth algebraic subgroup, N its normalizer in G, \(\varphi : \mathrm{G}\times\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\) the morphism defined by \(\varphi(g,h) = \mathrm{ad}(g)h = ghg^{-1}\), \(\psi : \mathrm{X} = \mathrm{G}\times^{\mathrm{N}}\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\) the morphism deduced from \(\varphi\) by passage to the quotient (cf. Section 1), and \(a \in \mathrm{H}(k)\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(\varphi\) is smooth at \((e,a)\).

  2. (ii)

    \(\psi\) is étale at \((\overline{e},a)\), and N is smooth over \(k\).

  3. (iii)

    \((\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h})^{\mathrm{ad}(a)} = 0\) (where \(\mathfrak{g}, \mathfrak{h}\) are the Lie algebras of G, H).

These conditions imply \(\mathrm{H}^0 = \mathrm{N}^0\).

It is known that the smoothness of \(\varphi\) (which is a morphism of smooth \(k\)-preschemes) at a point rational over \(k\) is equivalent to the surjectivity of the tangent map at that point. Now an immediate computation shows that this tangent map can be written (by means of the usual identifications of the tangent spaces at points of G and of H with the Lie algebra of G and of H) \[ d\varphi(\xi,\eta) = (\mathrm{id} - \mathrm{ad}(a))\cdot\xi + \eta, \] regarded as a map from \(\mathfrak{g}\times\mathfrak{h}\) to \(\mathfrak{g}\). Surjectivity is therefore equivalent to the surjectivity of \((\mathrm{id}-\mathrm{ad}(a))\) on \(\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}\), i.e. to (iii). Now (iii) implies a fortiori \((\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h})^{\mathrm{H}} = 0\), i.e. (cf. 2.0, where the connectedness hypothesis made at the beginning of the section is unnecessary) \(\mathrm{H}^0 = \mathrm{N}^0\). From this we deduce that N is smooth, and \(\dim \mathrm{H} = \dim \mathrm{N}\), whence \(\dim \mathrm{X} = \dim \mathrm{G}\). Now since \(q : \mathrm{G}\times\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{X}\) is flat and \(\psi = \varphi\circ q\) smooth at \((e,a)\), it follows that \(\psi\) is smooth at \(q(e,a) = (\overline{e},a)\), hence étale at that point for reasons of dimension. So we have proved (i) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii); on the other hand (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i), since the smoothness of N implies that of \(q\). Q.E.D.

Let us finally prove (vi) \(\Rightarrow\) (i), which, together with the implications already established, will prove the theorem. Suppose first that G is affine. Let U be a non-empty open subset of G such that \(x \in \mathrm{U}(k)\) implies that \(x\) is contained in a conjugate of H. Let C be a Cartan subgroup of G. Using the implication (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (iv) for C in place of H (this is Borel's "density theorem"), it follows that one can find a conjugate of C which meets U, so we may assume that \(\mathrm{U} \cap \mathrm{C} \neq \emptyset\), i.e. that there exists a non-empty open subset V of C such that for every \(x \in \mathrm{V}(k)\), \(x\) is contained in a conjugate of H. Write C as a product \[ \mathrm{C} = \mathrm{T}\cdot\mathrm{C}_u \] where T is the maximal torus of C (which is a maximal torus of G) and \(\mathrm{C}_u\) the unipotent part of C, T being in the center of C (BIBLE 6 th. 2). We may further assume that \(k\) is of infinite transcendence degree over its prime subfield, which allows us to find an element \(t\) of \(\mathrm{T}(k)\) which belongs to the projection of V onto T (which is a non-empty open subset of T), i.e. \(t\cdot\mathrm{C}_u \cap \mathrm{V} \neq \emptyset\), and such that \(t\) "generates" T. Since every algebraic subgroup of G containing a product \(t\cdot u\) (\(t \in \mathrm{T}(k), u \in \mathrm{C}_u(k)\)) contains both factors (BIBLE 4 th. 3), it follows, with the preceding choice of \(t\), and taking \(t\cdot u \in \mathrm{V}(k)\), that there exists a conjugate of H containing \(t\), hence T. So we may already assume that we have \[ \mathrm{T} \subset \mathrm{H}. \] If W is the open subset of \(\mathrm{C}_u\) which is the inverse image of V under \(u \mapsto t\cdot u\), we thus see that for every element \(x\) of \((\mathrm{T}\cdot\mathrm{W})(k)\), there exists a conjugate of H containing T and \(x\). As we have already remarked, such a conjugate is of the form \(\mathrm{int}(g)\cdot\mathrm{H}\), where \(g \in \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})(k)\). Consider then the morphism \[ f : \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T}) \times \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] defined by \(f(g,h) = \mathrm{int}(g)\cdot h = ghg^{-1}\); then the image of \(f\) contains \(\mathrm{T}\cdot\mathrm{W}\), hence, since \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})\) is a finite union of translates \(\mathrm{C}\cdot g_i\) (where \(g_i \in \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})(k)\)) because C is of finite index in \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})\), it follows that there exists a dense open subset \(\mathrm{V}'\) of \(\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{T}\cdot\mathrm{C}_u\) which is contained in the image of \((\mathrm{C}\cdot g_i)\times\mathrm{H}\) under \(f\). Replacing H by \(\mathrm{int}(g_i)\cdot\mathrm{H}\) if necessary, we may assume \(g_i = e\), i.e. \(f(\mathrm{C}\times\mathrm{H}) \supset \mathrm{V}'\). Hence for every \(u \in \mathrm{V}'(k)\), there exist \(v \in \mathrm{C}(k)\) and \(h \in \mathrm{H}(k)\) such that \[ v^{-1}hv = u \qquad \text{whence} \qquad vuv^{-1} \in \mathrm{H}(k), \] whence, setting \(\mathrm{C}' = \mathrm{C}\cap\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm{H}}(\mathrm{T})\), \(vuv^{-1} \in \mathrm{C}'\), whence \(\mathrm{int}(v)\cdot\mathrm{C}' \to u\). This proves that the union of the conjugates of \(\mathrm{C}'\) in C (by elements of \(\mathrm{C}(k)\)) is dense, which implies (as we saw for the case of the pair \((\mathrm{G},\mathrm{H})\) instead of \((\mathrm{C},\mathrm{C}')\)) that \(\mathrm{C}'\) is of finite index in its normalizer in C. By virtue of BIBLE 7, lemma of Section 1, it follows that \(\mathrm{C}' = \mathrm{C}\), hence \(\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{H}\), which proves (vi) \(\Rightarrow\) (i) when G is affine.
In the general case, we proceed by induction on \(n = \dim \mathrm{G}\), the assertion being trivial if \(n = 0\). Let Z be the center of G, and let us distinguish two cases:

1°) \(\dim \mathrm{Z}\cap\mathrm{H} > 0\); then, setting \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z}\cap\mathrm{H}\), we have \(\dim \mathrm{G}' < n\); on the other hand, hypothesis (vi) on H implies the same condition for the image \(\mathrm{H}'\) of H in \(\mathrm{G}'\), so \(\mathrm{H}'\) contains a Cartan subgroup \(\mathrm{C}'\) of \(\mathrm{G}'\), hence H contains the inverse image C of \(\mathrm{C}'\), which is a Cartan subgroup by virtue of XII 6.6 e).

2°) \(\dim \mathrm{Z}\cap\mathrm{H} = 0\), so the canonical morphism \(\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z}\) is a finite morphism, and since \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z}\) is affine by virtue of XII 6.1, it follows that H is affine, hence every homomorphism from H to an abelian variety is zero (and indeed every morphism of preschemes from H to an abelian variety is zero): this follows from the fact that a connected smooth affine algebraic group over an algebraically closed field is a rational variety, or simply that it is the union of its Borel subgroups (BIBLE 6 th. 5 b)); now it follows very easily from the structure theorems BIBLE 6.2 and 6.3 that a solvable connected smooth affine group is a rational variety. Let us now use Chevalley's structure theorem for G, according to which G is an extension of an abelian variety A by a smooth affine group. Then the image of H in A is zero, H being affine; on the other hand it is equal to A, since the union of its conjugates in A must be dense. Hence \(\mathrm{A} = 0\), so G is affine, and we are reduced to the case already treated. This completes the proof of 2.1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3

Suppose the equivalent conditions of 2.1 are satisfied.

  1. a)

    Let \(k(\mathrm{X})\) (resp. \(k(\mathrm{G})\)) be the field of rational functions of X (resp. G); then \(k(\mathrm{X})\) is a finite separable extension of \(k(\mathrm{G})\). Denote by \(d\) its degree.

  2. b)

    Let T be a maximal torus of G contained in H (which exists by the form 2.1 (ii)), and let C be the corresponding Cartan subgroup of G. Then \(\mathrm{C} \subset \mathrm{H}\). Moreover, \(\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})\) is a smooth subgroup of G and \(\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})\cap\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{H}) = \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{H})}(\mathrm{T})\) is a smooth subgroup of it, of finite index equal to \(d\) (defined in a)). The number of conjugates of H containing a given maximal torus or a given Cartan subgroup is equal to \(d\).

  3. c)

    Let U be the largest open subset of G such that \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) induces a morphism \(\psi^{-1}(\mathrm{U}) \to \mathrm{U}\) which is finite and étale. Then U is a dense open subset, and for \(g \in \mathrm{G}(k)\), we have \(g \in \mathrm{U}(k)\) if and only if there exist exactly \(d\) conjugates of H containing \(g\), or again if and only if there exist at least \(d\) distinct conjugates \(\mathrm{H}_i\) of H containing \(g\) such that for every \(i\), \((\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}_i)^{\mathrm{ad}(g)} = 0\) (where \(\mathfrak{h}_i = \mathrm{Lie}(\mathrm{H}_i)\)).

Assertion a) comes from the fact that \(\psi\) is generically étale (which was stated at the end of 2.1); this also implies that the open set U introduced in c) is non-empty, i.e. dense, as well as the two characterizations stated for the elements of \(\mathrm{U}(k)\) (taking into account that \(\psi\) is separated, X integral and G integral and normal, SGA 1, I 10.11, and the fact that \((\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}_i)^{\mathrm{ad}(g)} = 0\) means that \(\psi\) is étale at the point \(x_i\) of \(\psi^{-1}(g)\) corresponding to \(\mathrm{H}_i\)). If H contains the maximal torus T of G, then the centralizers of T in H and G have the same dimension, and are smooth and connected (XII 6.6 b)), hence are equal, which proves that \(\mathrm{C} \subset \mathrm{H}\). Moreover, it is known that the normalizer of T in a smooth group containing it is smooth (XI, 2.4 bis), so \(\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})\) and \(\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{H})}(\mathrm{T})\) are smooth (N.B. it was pointed out, at the end of the statement of 2.1, that \(\mathrm{N} = \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{H})\) is smooth); moreover \(\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{N}}(\mathrm{T})\) contains C, which is of finite index in \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})\), hence it is of finite index in \(\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{T})\). Using the conjugation theorem for the maximal tori of H, one sees that the index in question is equal to the number of conjugates of H containing T, or what amounts to the same thing, containing C. Now since the union of the conjugates of C in G is dense (by virtue of 2.1 (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (vi) applied to C instead of H), and the open set U defined in c) is obviously stable under inner automorphisms, we see that \(\mathrm{C}\cap\mathrm{U} \neq \emptyset\). Proceeding as in the proof of the implication (vi) \(\Rightarrow\) (i) of 2.1, we conclude that (after a harmless change of base field, if necessary) there exists a \(g \in (\mathrm{C}\cap\mathrm{U})(k)\) such that every conjugate of H containing \(g\) contains T, and consequently also C. Hence the conjugates of H containing C are those containing \(g\), and since \(g \in \mathrm{U}(k)\), their number is equal to \(d\), which completes the proof of b). Note that we have in fact established that the set of conjugates of H containing T is a homogeneous set under the group of rational points of \[ \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}) = \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})/\mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}), \] which proves in particular that \[ d \leqslant \text{order of the Weyl group of G}. \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4

With the notation of 2.1, the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) is a birational morphism.

  2. (ii)

    There is one and only one conjugate of H containing a given Cartan subgroup of G.

  3. (iii)

    H contains a Cartan subgroup C of G, and \(\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{H}) \supset \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{C})\).

  4. (iv)

    There exists a non-empty open subset V of G such that \(g \in \mathrm{V}(k)\) implies that \(g\) is contained in exactly one conjugate of H.

This is clear thanks to 2.1 and 2.3.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.5

Suppose the conditions of 2.4 are satisfied, and let \(g \in \mathrm{G}(k)\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(g \in \mathrm{U}(k)\), where U is defined in 2.3 c), i.e. \(g\) is contained in one and only one conjugate of H.

  2. (ii)

    The set of conjugates of H containing \(g\) is finite and non-empty.

  3. (iii)

    The scheme \(\psi^{-1}(g)\) "of conjugates of H containing \(g\)" contains an isolated point.

  4. (iv)

    There exists a conjugate \(\mathrm{H}'\) of H containing \(g\), and we have \((\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}')^{\mathrm{ad}(g)} = 0\), where \(\mathfrak{h}' = \mathrm{Lie}(\mathrm{H}')\).

Finally, U is also the largest open subset of G such that \(\psi\) induces an isomorphism \(\psi^{-1}(\mathrm{U}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{U}\).

The equivalence of (i), (ii), (iii), as well as the last assertion, follow from the "Main Theorem"3 applied to the birational morphism \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\), taking into account that G is normal. The equivalence of these conditions with (iv) follows at once from the last assertion of 2.1 characterizing the set of elements of X at which \(\psi\) is étale.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.6

Let G be a connected smooth algebraic group over an algebraically closed field \(k\), C a Cartan subgroup, associated with a maximal torus T, \(\mathrm{N} = \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{C}) = \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})\) (cf. XII 8.4); let \(\mathrm{X} = \mathrm{G}\times^{\mathrm{N}}\mathrm{C}\), where N acts on the left factor G by right translations, and on the right factor C by inner automorphisms, and let \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) be the canonical morphism.

  1. a)

    The morphism \(\psi\) is birational.

  2. b)

    Let U be the largest open subset of G such that \(\psi\) induces an isomorphism \(\psi^{-1}(\mathrm{U}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{U}\) (cf. 2.5). Let \[ \rho = \rho_{\nu}(\mathrm{G}) = \dim \mathrm{C} \] be the nilpotent rank of G. Then for every \(g \in \mathrm{G}(k)\), the multiplicity of the eigenvalue 1 in \(\mathrm{ad}(g)\) acting on \(\mathfrak{g}\) is at least equal to \(\rho\), and for it to be equal to \(\rho\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(g \in \mathrm{U}(k)\).

Proof. Since condition 2.1 (i) is satisfied, one can apply 2.4 (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i), which establishes (a). In BIBLE 7 (in the case where G is affine) the points of \(\mathrm{U}(k)\) are called the regular points of \(\mathrm{G}(k)\), and we shall follow this terminology, calling U the open set of regular points of G. (N.B. The proof given in BIBLE of the fact that the set in question is itself open is incorrect, but we have obtained it in the present exposé under more general conditions.)

Let us prove b), and to this end, introduce for every \(g \in \mathrm{G}(k)\) the characteristic polynomial \[ \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{ad}(g),t) = t^n + c_1(g)t^{n-1} + \cdots + c_n(g); \] one sees at once (replacing \(k\) by an arbitrary algebra over \(k\)) that the \(c_i(g)\) come from well-determined sections \[ c_i \in \Gamma(\mathrm{G}, \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{G}}). \] When \(g \in \mathrm{G}(k)\) is an element contained in a Cartan subgroup (for instance a regular element), which we may assume to be C, then by 2.5 (iv) we see that \((\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{c})^{\mathrm{ad}(g)} = 0\) if and only if \(g\) is regular (where \(\mathfrak{c}\) denotes the Lie algebra of C); on the other hand, since C is nilpotent, one sees at once that \(\mathrm{ad}_{\mathfrak{c}}(g)\) has 1 as its only eigenvalue, which proves that the multiplicity of the eigenvalue 1 in \(\mathrm{ad}_{\mathfrak{g}}(g)\) is \(\geqslant \rho\), and exactly equal to \(\dim \mathrm{C} = \rho\) if and only if \(g\) is regular. In particular, the polynomial above is divisible by \((t-1)^{\rho}\). Since the relation of divisibility by \((t-1)^{\rho}\) is expressed by linear relations (with integer coefficients) among the coefficients of the polynomial, and these relations are satisfied for \(g \in \mathrm{U}(k)\), U being a dense open set, it follows (G being reduced) that they are satisfied for every \(g\); in fact we have a relation \[ (\dagger) \qquad t^n + c_1t^{n-1} + c_0 = (t-1)^{\rho}(t^{n-\rho} + b_1t^{n-\rho-1} + \cdots + b_{n-\rho}) \] in the ring of polynomials over \(\Gamma(\mathrm{G}, \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{G}})\); in particular, for every \(g \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{K})\), \(\mathrm{ad}(g)\) has the eigenvalue 1 with multiplicity at least \(\rho\). Moreover, we have seen that equality holds if \(g\) is regular; let us prove the converse. To this end, suppose first that G is affine, and write \(g\) as the product \[ g = g_sg_u \] of its semisimple part and its unipotent part (BIBLE 4 Section 4); then \[ \mathrm{ad}(g) = \mathrm{ad}(g_s)\,\mathrm{ad}(g_u) \] is the analogous decomposition of \(\mathrm{ad}(g)\) (loc. cit. cor. to th. 3), and consequently \(\mathrm{ad}(g)\) and \(\mathrm{ad}(g_s)\) have the same eigenvalues (counted with their multiplicities); in particular the eigenvalue 1 occurs with the same multiplicity in \(\mathrm{ad}(g)\) and in \(\mathrm{ad}(g_s)\).

On the other hand, by virtue of BIBLE 7 th. 2 cor. 1, \(g\) is regular if and only if \(g_s\) is. Hence, to prove b), we may assume \(g = g_s\), i.e. \(g\) semisimple, hence contained in a maximal torus by virtue of BIBLE 6 th. 5 c), and a fortiori in a Cartan subgroup, a case which has already been treated. This proves b) in the case G affine. In the general case, let \(\mathrm{Z} = \mathrm{Centr}(\mathrm{G})_{\text{réd}}\); then by virtue of XII 6.6 e) the Cartan subgroups of G are the inverse images of those of \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z}\), so \(g\) is regular in G if and only if its image \(g'\) in \(\mathrm{G}'\) is regular in \(\mathrm{G}'\). On the other hand, since Z is smooth, the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}'\) of \(\mathrm{G}'\) is none other than \(\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{z}\), where \(\mathfrak{z} = \mathrm{Lie}(\mathrm{Z})\), and \(\mathrm{ad}(g')\) is none other than \(\mathrm{ad}(g)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{z}}\), so the multiplicity of the eigenvalue 1 in \(\mathrm{ad}(g)\) is equal to \(d = \dim \mathrm{Z}\) plus the multiplicity of the eigenvalue 1 in \(\mathrm{ad}(g')\), from which it follows at once that the former is equal to the nilpotent rank of G if and only if the latter is equal to the nilpotent rank of \(\mathrm{G}'\). Thus we are reduced to the case of \(\mathrm{G}'\); now \(\mathrm{G}'\) being affine by virtue of XII 6.1, this case has already been treated. This completes the proof of 2.6.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.7

With the notation of the preceding proof4, let \[ b = 1 + b_1 + \cdots + b_{n-\rho} \in \Gamma(\mathrm{G}, \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{G}}). \] Then the open set of regular points of G is given by \[ \mathrm{U} = \mathrm{G}_b \] (the set of points of G at which \(b\) is invertible); in particular U is an affine open subset if G is affine.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.8

Let H be a connected smooth algebraic subgroup of G containing a Cartan subgroup of G.

  1. a)

    Let C be an algebraic subgroup of H. For C to be a Cartan subgroup of H, it is necessary and sufficient that it be a Cartan subgroup of G.

  2. b)

    Let \(g \in \mathrm{G}(k)\), and let \(d\) be the integer introduced in 2.3. For \(g\) to be a regular point of G, it is necessary and sufficient that there exist exactly \(d\) conjugates \(\mathrm{H}_i\) of H containing \(g\), and that for each \(i\), \(g\) be a regular element of \(\mathrm{H}_i\); or again, that there be at most \(d\) conjugates of H containing \(g\), and that \(g\) be regular in one of them. If this is the case, and if C is the unique Cartan subgroup of G containing \(g\), then the conjugates of H containing \(g\) are the conjugates of H containing C.

  3. c)

    Let \(g \in \mathrm{H}(k)\); for \(g\) to be regular in G, it is necessary and sufficient that it be regular in H, and that \[ (\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h})^{\mathrm{ad}(g)} = 0. \]

Let us prove a). Under either hypothesis on C, the unique maximal torus T of C is a maximal torus of H and of G (H having the same reductive rank as G); hence, since \(\mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm{H}}(\mathrm{T}) \subset \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})\) are smooth connected groups of the same dimension, they are equal, so it amounts to the same thing to say that C is equal to the one or to the other of these two groups, which proves a).

Let us prove b). Suppose first that \(g\) is regular for G, and let C be the unique Cartan subgroup of G containing \(g\); then by virtue of 2.3 b) there exist exactly \(d\) conjugates \(\mathrm{H}_i\) of H containing C. Since \((\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{c})^{\mathrm{ad}(g)} = 0\), i.e. \(\mathrm{ad}(g)\) has no eigenvalue \(+1\) in \(\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{c}\), we have a fortiori \((\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}_i)^{\mathrm{ad}(g)} = 0\), so by virtue of 2.3 c) there are exactly \(d\) conjugates of H containing \(g\), namely the \(\mathrm{H}_i\). For such an \(\mathrm{H}_i\), a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{H}_i\) containing \(g\) is a Cartan subgroup of G containing \(g\) by virtue of a), hence equal to C, which proves that \(g\) is regular in \(\mathrm{H}_i\).

Conversely, suppose that there exist at most \(d\) conjugates \(\mathrm{H}_i\) of H containing \(g\), and that \(g\) is regular in one of them, which we may assume equal to H. Let us prove that \(g\) is regular in G. Since \(g\) is regular in H, it is contained in a unique Cartan subgroup C of H; by a), this is a Cartan subgroup of G. Let \(\mathrm{C}'\) be a Cartan subgroup of G containing \(g\); let us prove \(\mathrm{C}' = \mathrm{C}\) (which will prove that \(g\) is regular in C). Indeed, by virtue of 2.3 b) there exist exactly \(d\) conjugates of H containing \(\mathrm{C}'\), and since these contain \(g\), they are necessarily the \(\mathrm{H}_i\), so the \(\mathrm{H}_i\), and in particular H, contain \(\mathrm{C}'\). Hence C, \(\mathrm{C}'\) are two Cartan subgroups of H (by virtue of a)) containing the same regular element \(g\) of H, hence they are equal. Q.E.D.

Let us prove c): denoting by \( u(u)\) the nullity5 of \(\mathrm{id} - u\), for an endomorphism of a finite-dimensional vector space, we shall have \[ u(\mathrm{ad}(g)_{\mathfrak{g}}) = u(\mathrm{ad}(g)_{\mathfrak{h}}) + u(\mathrm{ad}(g)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}}), \] now the two terms of the right-hand side are respectively \(\geqslant\) the nilpotent rank of H (equal to the nilpotent rank \( ho\) of G by virtue of a)) and \(\geqslant 0\), hence we have \( u(\mathrm{ad}(g)_{\mathfrak{g}}) = ho\) if and only if \( u(\mathrm{ad}(g)_{\mathfrak{h}}) = ho\) and \( u(\mathrm{ad}(g)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}}) = 0\), i.e. \(g\) is regular in G if and only if \(g\) is regular in H and \(\mathrm{ad}(g)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}}\) has no non-zero invariants. Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 2.9

In the statement of 2.1, one cannot weaken condition (iii) by supposing only that H contains a maximal torus and is of finite index in its normalizer, even if one requires moreover that this normalizer be smooth, i.e. that \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{N}^0\), and even when G is affine and solvable. An example is provided by the group G of matrices of the form \[ g = \begin{pmatrix} t & a & c \\ 0 & 1 & b \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \] and the subgroup H of matrices of the preceding form with \(b = c = 0\) (N.B. the Cartan subgroup of G here consists of the matrices \(g\) with \(a = c = 0\)).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 2.10

Let G be a smooth algebraic group over \(k\), and H a smooth algebraic subgroup, but let us no longer suppose that H and G are connected. Suppose that \(\mathrm{H}^0\) contains a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}^0\). Then \((\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h})^{\mathrm{H}} \subset (\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h})^{\mathrm{H}^0} = 0\), so \(\mathrm{H}^0 = \mathrm{N}^0\) (N being the normalizer of H); in particular N is smooth. However, one easily constructs examples, with G connected, in which H has a connected component \(\mathrm{H}_i\) such that for no \(h \in \mathrm{H}_i(k)\) do we have \((\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h})^{\mathrm{ad}(h)} = 0\), i.e. the morphism \((g,h) \to \mathrm{ad}(g)\cdot h\) from \(\mathrm{G}\times\mathrm{H}_i\) to G is not étale (nor even quasi-finite) at any point (take, for example, for H the normalizer of the maximal torus in \(\mathrm{SL}(2)_k\)). Likewise, even if the image \(\mathrm{H}'\) of H in the finite group \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{G}^0\) is equal to \(\mathrm{G}'\), it is not necessarily true that the union of the conjugates of H in G is dense (take for G the semidirect product of \(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\) with \(\mathrm{G}^0 = \mathrm{SL}(2)_k\), on which it acts by "symmetry", and for H the semidirect product \(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\cdot\mathrm{T}\), where T is a maximal torus of \(\mathrm{G}^0\)). On the other hand, if one does not suppose a priori that \(\mathrm{H}^0\) contains a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}^0\), but that the union of the conjugates of H in G is dense, then \(\mathrm{H}^0\) necessarily contains a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}^0\): to verify this, we may obviously suppose G connected, and it suffices to go through the proof of 2.1 (vi) \(\Rightarrow\) (i) again, which is valid without supposing H connected.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: i.e., the dimension of its nil-space.

pages.title: 3. Case of an arbitrary base prescheme

blocks.html: 3. Case of an arbitrary base prescheme

Suppose first that we are over a base field \(k\), not necessarily algebraically closed. Since conditions 2.1 (i bis), (iv bis), (v), (vi) are invariant under extension of the base field, one sees by passing to the algebraic closure \(\overline{k}\) of \(k\) that they are equivalent to one another, and are equivalent to the fact that \(\mathrm{H}_{\overline{k}}\) contains a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{k}}\). When this condition is satisfied, it will still be true (with the notation of 2.3) that \(k(\mathrm{X})\) is a finite separable extension of \(k(\mathrm{G})\), of degree \(d\) independent of any extension of the base. If U is the largest open subset of G such that \(\psi\) induces a morphism \(\psi^{-1}(\mathrm{U}) \to \mathrm{U}\) which is finite and étale, then the formation of U commutes with extension of the base field. If \(\psi\) is birational, then U is also the largest open subset of G such that \(\psi\) induces an isomorphism \(\psi^{-1}(\mathrm{U}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{U}\), and if, then, \(g \in \mathrm{U}(k)\), there exists one and only one subgroup \(\mathrm{H}'\) of G, conjugate to H over the algebraic closure \(\overline{k}\) of \(k\), such that \(\mathrm{H}' \ni g\).

A point \(g \in \mathrm{G}(k)\) is called regular if it is regular as an element of \(\mathrm{G}(\overline{k}) = \mathrm{G}_{\overline{k}}(\overline{k})\). More generally, the construction of 2.7 gives us an open subset of G, whose formation commutes with any extension of the base field, called the open set of regular points of G, which is also characterized by the fact that for every algebraically closed extension K of \(k\) and every point \(g \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{K})\), \(g\) is a regular point of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{K}}\) if and only if \(g \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{K})\). If \(g \in \mathrm{U}(k)\)6, one sees that \(g\) is contained in one and only one Cartan subgroup of G, as we shall show under more general conditions below.

Let G be a group prescheme which is smooth, separated, of finite type, with connected fibers over the prescheme S, and consider the functor \(\mathscr{C} : (\mathbf{Sch})^{\circ}_{/\mathrm{S}} \to (\mathbf{Ens})\) defined by \(\mathscr{C}(\mathrm{S}') =\) the set of Cartan subgroups (XII 3.1) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\). Suppose that this functor is representable by a prescheme smooth over S; we give in XV a necessary and sufficient condition for this to be the case, but we already know that this hypothesis is satisfied if G is affine over S of locally constant reductive rank (XII 3.3), or more generally if G admits, locally for the fpqc topology, a maximal torus (XII 7.1 a)), for example if S is the spectrum of a field. Let X be the Cartan subgroup of the \(\mathscr{C}\)-group prescheme \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{C}}\), the "universal Cartan subgroup" of G. As a prescheme over S, X therefore represents the functor \[ \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}') = \text{set of pairs } (\mathrm{C},g), \; \mathrm{C} \text{ a Cartan subgroup of } \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'} \text{ and } g \text{ a section of } \mathrm{C} \text{ over } \mathrm{S}'. \] Consider the canonical projection morphism \((\mathrm{C},g) \mapsto g\) \[ \psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}. \] We then have the

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.1

Under the preceding conditions on G, and with the preceding notation, let U be the set of \(g \in \mathrm{G}\) such that \(g\) is a regular element of its fiber \(\mathrm{G}_s\). Then U is open, and it is also the largest open subset U of G such that \(\psi\) induces an isomorphism \[ \psi^{-1}(\mathrm{U}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{U}. \]

Let us first prove that U is open. From the hypothesis that \(\mathscr{C}\) is representable as a prescheme smooth over S, since its structural morphism is obviously surjective, one concludes at once that G admits, locally for the étale topology, a Cartan subgroup, and that the nilpotent rank of the fibers of G is locally constant. The same holds for the dimension of the fibers of G, and after localizing on S if necessary, we may assume that both are constant, say \(\rho\) and \(n\). Consider then the Killing polynomial \[ \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{G}}(t) = t^n + c_1t^{n-1} + \cdots + c_n \in \mathrm{A}[t], \qquad \text{where } \mathrm{A} = \Gamma(\mathrm{G}, \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{G}}). \] The restriction of this polynomial to the fibers \(\mathrm{G}_s\) of G, and in particular to the fibers at the maximal points of S, is divisible by \((t-1)^{\rho}\), which is expressed by the fact that certain linear combinations with integer coefficients of the \(c_i\) vanish on the fibers \(\mathrm{G}_s\). When S is reduced (which we may assume in order to establish that U is open), it follows that they themselves vanish, hence that the Killing polynomial itself is divisible by \((t-1)^{\rho}\), say \[ \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{G}}(t) = (t-1)^{\rho}(t^{n-\rho} + b_1t^{n-r-1} + \cdots + b_{n-\rho}). \] Let \(b\) be the sum of the coefficients \(b_0 = 1, b_1, \ldots, b_{n-\rho}\) of the second factor; then by virtue of 2.7 applied to the fibers of G, one sees that \[ \mathrm{U} = \mathrm{G}_b, \] which indeed proves that U is open.

To prove that \(\psi^{-1}(\mathrm{U}) \to \mathrm{U}\) is an isomorphism, one is reduced by SGA 1, I 5.7 to checking it fiber by fiber, which brings us back to the case of a base field, which we may assume algebraically closed. Then there exists a Cartan subgroup C of G, and if N is its normalizer, C is identified, by the conjugation theorem XII 7.1 a) and b), with \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\), and the morphism \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) considered here is none other than the one defined in Section 2. One then concludes by 2.6 b). The same reasoning also shows that U is the largest open subset of G such that \(\psi\) induces an isomorphism \(\psi^{-1}(\mathrm{U}) \to \mathrm{U}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.2

Under the conditions of 3.1, let \(g\) be a regular section of G, i.e. such that for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(g(s)\) is a regular point of \(\mathrm{G}_s\). Then there exists one and only one Cartan subgroup C of G such that \(g\) is a section of C.

Indeed, the hypothesis on \(g\) means that \(g\) is a section of U, and the conclusion that there exists a unique section of X lifting it, which is merely another way of expressing that \(\psi^{-1}(\mathrm{U}) \to \mathrm{U}\) is an isomorphism.

Note now that the open subset \(\psi^{-1}(\mathrm{U})\) of the Cartan subgroup X of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{C}}\) is none other than the open subset of X consisting of the points of X that are regular in \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{C}}\) (by which we mean: regular in their fiber). One thus obtains a natural "fibration" of the dense open set U of regular points of G over the prescheme \(\mathscr{C}\), the fibers being dense open subsets of Cartan subgroups of the fibers of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathscr{C}}\) (namely, the open sets of points regular in G). One finds, for example, the following result (which will be considerably sharpened in the next exposé):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.3

Let G be a connected smooth algebraic group over the field \(k\), and \(\mathscr{T}\) the scheme of maximal tori of G (\(\simeq\) the scheme of Cartan subgroups of G). Then the field of functions \(k(\mathrm{G})\) of G is isomorphic to the field of functions of a nilpotent affine connected smooth algebraic group C over the field of functions \(k(\mathscr{T})\) of \(\mathscr{T}\), namely \(\mathrm{C} = \)"the generic Cartan subgroup of G". If G is affine of unipotent rank zero, i.e. if the Cartan subgroups of \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{\mathrm{K}}}\) are tori, then \(k(\mathrm{G})\) is a unirational extension of \(k(\mathscr{T})\).

Of course, by the generic Cartan subgroup of G we mean (by abuse of language) the Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_{k(\mathscr{T})}\) which is the generic fiber of X over \(\mathscr{T}\). It only remains to prove the last assertion of 3.3, which is contained in the following well-known result (due to Chevalley):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.4

Let \(k\) be a field, T a torus over \(k\), and \(k(\mathrm{T})\) the field of rational functions on T; then \(k(\mathrm{T})\) is a unirational extension of \(k\), i.e. is contained in a purely transcendental extension of \(k\).

Indeed, let \(k'\) be a finite separable extension of \(k\) which splits7 T (X 1.4); then \(\mathrm{T}\otimes_k k'\) is a rational variety, i.e. admits a dense open subset isomorphic to a dense open subset of affine space \(\mathbb{A}^n_{k'}\), hence \(\mathrm{T}' = \prod_{\mathrm{Spec}(k')/\mathrm{Spec}(k)} \mathrm{T}_{k'}/\mathrm{Spec}(k')\) is a rational variety (for it admits a dense open subset isomorphic to a dense open subset of \(\prod_{\mathrm{Spec}(k')/\mathrm{Spec}(k)} \mathbb{A}^n_{k'}\), which is isomorphic to affine space of dimension \(mn\) over \(k\), where \(m = [k':k]\)). Consider the norm homomorphism from \(\mathrm{T}'\) to T (defined whenever T is a commutative group scheme over \(k\)); the composite \(\mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{T}' \to \mathrm{T}\) is the \(m\)-th power map in T, hence dominant, hence \(\mathrm{T}' \to \mathrm{T}\) is dominant, which proves that T is unirational.

Let us return to the conditions of 3.1, assuming moreover that G admits, locally for the fpqc topology, a maximal torus (XII 7.1). Let Y be the maximal torus of the Cartan subgroup X of G, so that the morphism \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) induces a morphism \(\mathrm{Y} \to \mathrm{G}\) whose image consists, set-theoretically, of the semisimple elements of the fibers of G (XII 8). Finally, it follows from 3.1 that the restriction of \(\psi\) to the open subset \(\mathrm{Y}^{\text{rég}}\) of regular points of Y induces a closed immersion \[ \mathrm{Y}^{\text{rég}} \to \mathrm{U} = \mathrm{G}^{\text{rég}}. \] Making explicit the meaning of \(\mathrm{Z} = \mathrm{Y}^{\text{rég}}\) as a functor over S, one finds:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.5

Let G be an S-group prescheme which is smooth, separated, of finite type and with connected fibers over the prescheme S, admitting locally for the fpqc topology a maximal torus. Let \(\mathrm{Z} : (\mathbf{Sch})^{\circ}_{/\mathrm{S}} \to (\mathbf{Ens})\) be the functor defined by \[ \mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{S}') = \text{set of regular sections of } \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'} \text{ over } \mathrm{S}' \text{ which are contained in a maximal torus of } \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'} \] Then Z is representable by a closed subprescheme, smooth over S, of the open set \(\mathrm{U} = \mathrm{G}^{\text{rég}}\) of G introduced in 3.1.
To finish, let us note the following result, which sharpens the density theorem 2.1 (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (vi):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.6

Under the conditions of 3.5, let C be a Cartan subgroup of G, and consider the morphism \[ \varphi : \mathrm{Z}\times\mathrm{C} \to \mathrm{G} \] defined by \(\varphi(g,h) = \mathrm{ad}(g)h = ghg^{-1}\). Then \(\varphi\) is dominant.

It obviously suffices to prove this fiber by fiber, which brings us back to the case where S is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field. Let T be the maximal torus of C, \(t_0\) an element of \(\mathrm{T}(k)\) regular in G, and \(c_0\) an element of \(\mathrm{C}(k)\) regular in G; consider \(\varphi^{-1}(\varphi(t_0,c_0))\), whose rational points over \(k\) are the pairs \((t,c)\), with \(t \in \mathrm{Z}(k)\), \(c \in \mathrm{C}(k)\), such that \[ \mathrm{ad}(t)c = \mathrm{ad}(t_0)c_0 \qquad \text{i.e.} \qquad c = \mathrm{ad}(t^{-1}t_0)c_0, \] which are therefore in one-to-one correspondence with the \(t \in \mathrm{Z}(k)\) such that \(\mathrm{ad}(t^{-1}t_0)c_0 \in \mathrm{C}\), or what amounts to the same thing, \(c_0\) being regular, such that \(t^{-1}t_0 \in \mathrm{N}\) (the normalizer of C), i.e. \(t \in \mathrm{N}\). One obtains an open and closed part of this fiber by restricting to the \(t \in \mathrm{Z}(k)\) such that \(t \in \mathrm{C}(k)\). So we have found a connected component of \(\varphi^{-1}\varphi(t_0,c_0)\) isomorphic to T (N.B. that the preceding set-theoretic reasoning indeed gives an isomorphism of schemes is seen by replacing points with values in \(k\) by points with values in an arbitrary \(k\)-prescheme), so the generic fiber of \(\varphi\) is of dimension \(\leqslant \dim \mathrm{T}\), so \(\mathrm{Im}(\varphi)\) is of dimension \(\geqslant \dim \mathrm{Z}\times\mathrm{C} - \dim \mathrm{T} = \dim \mathrm{Z} + \dim \mathrm{C} - \dim \mathrm{T}\); now we have \(\dim \mathrm{Z} = \dim \mathrm{Y} = \dim \mathscr{C} + \dim \mathrm{T} = \dim \mathrm{G} - \dim \mathrm{C} + \dim \mathrm{T}\), whence finally \(\dim \mathrm{Im}(\varphi) \geqslant \dim \mathrm{G}\), so \(\varphi\) is dominant. Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 3.7

Note that the reasoning shows in addition that the connected component at \((t_0,c_0)\) of the fiber of \(\varphi(t_0,c_0)\) is isomorphic to T, in particular is smooth over \(k\), and has the same dimension as the generic fiber, which implies that \(\varphi\) is in fact smooth at \((t_0,c_0)\) (something one should also be able to verify by computing the tangent map). It follows that, under the conditions of 3.6, the induced morphism \(\mathrm{Z}\times_{\mathrm{S}}\mathrm{C}^{\text{rég}} \to \mathrm{G}^{\text{rég}}\) (where we have set \(\mathrm{C}^{\text{rég}} = \mathrm{C}\cap\mathrm{G}^{\text{rég}}\)) is a smooth morphism. One sees likewise that the analogous morphism \(\mathrm{Z}\times\mathrm{T}^{\text{rég}} \to \mathrm{Z}\) (where T is a maximal torus of G) is smooth; more generally, for every invariant connected smooth algebraic subgroup H of C containing a regular element \(c_0\) of \(\mathrm{G}(k)\), the image of \(\mathrm{Z}\times\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\) is dense in that of \(\mathrm{G}\times\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\).

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: correction of G(k) to U(k).
N.D.E.: the text has the anglicism "splitte"; read: déploie (?), or: "over which T is split".

pages.title: 4. Lie algebras over a field: rank, regular elements, Cartan subalgebras

blocks.html: 4. Lie algebras over a field: rank, regular elements, Cartan subalgebras

In the remainder of this exposé, we take up again the theory developed by Chevalley in his book "Théorie des Groupes de Lie III" (Act. Sc. Ind. 1226, Paris 1955), the technique of schemes allowing us to eliminate the hypothesis of characteristic zero. We begin by recalling in the present section certain well-known notions and results.

Let \(\mathfrak{g}\) be a Lie algebra over a ring \(k\). For every \(a \in \mathfrak{g}\), we denote by \(\mathrm{ad}(a)\) the endomorphism \[ \mathrm{ad}(a)\cdot x = [a,x] \] of \(\mathfrak{g}\), which is a derivation of the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}\). Now for every derivation D of the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}\), the nil-space of D, i.e. the union of the kernels of the iterates of D, is a Lie subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\), as one sees from the Leibniz formula \[ \mathrm{D}^m([x,y]) = \sum_{0\leqslant p\leqslant m} \binom{m}{p} [\mathrm{D}^px, \mathrm{D}^{m-p}y]. \] We shall set \[ \mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g}) = \text{ nil-space of } \mathrm{ad}(a) = \bigcup_{m\geqslant 0} \mathrm{Ker}\,\mathrm{ad}(a)^m; \] when no confusion is to be feared, we shall denote it simply by \(\mathrm{Nil}(a)\), and call it the nil-space of \(a\) (in \(\mathfrak{g}\)).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.1

For every \(a \in \mathfrak{g}\), its nil-space \(\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\) is a Lie subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\), equal to its own normalizer.

It remains to prove that it is its own normalizer, i.e. that every element of \(\mathfrak{g}/\mathrm{Nil}\) annihilated by the adjoint representation of Nil in \(\mathfrak{g}/\mathrm{Nil}\) is zero, which is trivial (for every element in this quotient annihilated by \(\mathrm{Ad}(a)\) is zero).

In the remainder of this section, we assume that \(k\) is a field, and \(\mathfrak{g}\) of finite dimension over \(k\). We shall denote by \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) the scheme over \(k\) defined by \(\mathfrak{g}\), whose points in the \(k\)-algebra A are the elements of \(\mathfrak{g}\otimes_k \mathrm{A}\). If \(a \in \mathfrak{g}\), the characteristic polynomial of \(\mathrm{ad}(a)\) is also called the characteristic polynomial or Killing polynomial of \(a\) in \(\mathfrak{g}\), say \[ \mathrm{P}_{\mathfrak{g}}(a,t) = t^n + c_1(a)t^{n-1} + \cdots + c_n(a), \] where \(n = \mathrm{rang}_k\,\mathfrak{g}\), and the \(c_i(a) \in k\). Taking this polynomial also for \(a \in \mathfrak{g}\otimes_k \mathrm{A}\)8, where A is an arbitrary \(k\)-algebra, one sees that the \(c_i(a)\) come from well-determined sections \(c_i\) of the structure sheaf of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\), i.e. from elements of the symmetric algebra \(\mathrm{A} = \mathrm{Sym}_k(\mathfrak{g}^{\vee})\), where \(\mathfrak{g}^{\vee}\) is the dual of the \(k\)-module \(\mathfrak{g}\). (When \(k\) is an infinite field, the \(c_i\) are determined by the knowledge of the corresponding polynomial functions \(\mathfrak{g} \to k\), but this is no longer so if \(k\) is a finite field.) Let \(r\) be the largest integer such that the Killing polynomial \[ \mathrm{P}_{\mathfrak{g}}(t) = t^n + c_1t^{n-1} + \cdots + c_n \in \mathrm{A}[t] \] is divisible by \(t^r\), i.e. we have: \[ \mathrm{P}_{\mathfrak{g}}(t) = t^n + c_1t^{n-1} + \ldots + c_{n-r}t^r, \qquad c_{n-r} \neq 0. \] The integer \(r\) is called the nilpotent rank of the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}\). It is invariant under extension of the base field.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.2

Let \(r\) be the nilpotent rank of \(\mathfrak{g}\), and let \(a \in \mathfrak{g}\). Then we have \[ \mathrm{rang}_k\,\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g}) \geqslant r, \] and equality holds if and only if we have \[ c_{n-r}(a) \neq 0. \] In that case, \(\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\) is a nilpotent Lie algebra (and we shall see in 5.7 b) the converse, when \(\mathfrak{g}\) is the Lie algebra of an algebraic group G smooth over \(k\)).

The first assertion is trivial, for by definition we have \(\mathrm{rang}_k\,\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g}) =\) the multiplicity of the zero root in \(\mathrm{P}_{\mathfrak{g}}(a,t)\). Let us prove that if \(c_{n-r}(a) \neq 0\), then \(\mathrm{Nil}(a)\) is nilpotent, which also means that for every \(x \in \mathrm{Nil}(a)\), \(\mathrm{ad}_{\mathrm{Nil}(a)}(x)\) is a nilpotent endomorphism. We may assume \(k\) algebraically closed; then, since \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathrm{Nil}(a)}\) is injective, there exists a non-empty open subset U of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathrm{Nil}(a))\) such that for every \(x \in \mathrm{U}(k)\), \(\mathrm{ad}(x)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathrm{Nil}(a)}\) is injective, hence \(\mathrm{Nil}(x) \subset \mathrm{Nil}(a)\); we may further assume U contained in the open set of points where \(c_{n-r}\) does not vanish (since this open set is non-empty by virtue of \(c_{n-r}(a) \neq 0\)), and then, \(\mathrm{Nil}(x)\) having the same dimension as \(\mathrm{Nil}(a)\), we shall have \(\mathrm{Nil}(x) = \mathrm{Nil}(a)\). Consequently, for every \(x \in \mathrm{U}(k)\), \(\mathrm{ad}(x)_{\mathrm{Nil}(a)}\) is nilpotent, and by the principle of prolongation of algebraic identities, this remains true for every \(x \in \mathrm{Nil}(a)\), so \(\mathrm{Nil}(a)\) is nilpotent.

One says that the element \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\) is regular if \(c_{n-r}(a) \neq 0\), i.e. if \(\mathrm{rang}_k(\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g}) = r\). When \(k\) is infinite, this therefore also means that \(\mathrm{rang}_k\,\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\) is the smallest possible (for \(a\) varying in \(\mathfrak{g}\)). In any case, the notion of a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}\) is invariant under extension of the base field, and the set of points of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) which are regular (i.e. which come from regular points of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) with values in a suitable extension of \(k\)) is open, being identical with \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})_{c_{n-r}}\) (the set of points where \(c_{n-r}\) is invertible).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3

Let \(a\) be a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}\), and \(\mathfrak{h}\) a Lie subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) containing \(a\). Then \(\mathfrak{h}\) is nilpotent if and only if \(\mathfrak{h} \subset \mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\); in particular, \(\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\) is a maximal nilpotent subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

Since \(\mathrm{Nil}(a)\) is nilpotent, the relation \(\mathfrak{h} \subset \mathrm{Nil}(a)\) indeed implies that \(\mathfrak{h}\) is nilpotent; conversely, if \(\mathfrak{h}\) is nilpotent, it is contained in the nil-space of its element \(a\), i.e. \(\mathfrak{h} \subset \mathrm{Nil}(a)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.4

Suppose \(k\) infinite. Let \(\mathfrak{d}\) be a Lie subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\). Consider the following conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(\mathfrak{d}\) is maximal nilpotent and contains a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

  2. (i bis)

    \(\mathfrak{d}\) is of the form \(\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\), where \(a\) is a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

  3. (ii)

    \(\mathfrak{d}\) is nilpotent and of the form \(\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\), where \(a \in \mathfrak{g}\).

  4. (ii bis)

    \(\mathfrak{d}\) is nilpotent, and there exists \(a \in \mathfrak{d}\) such that \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{d}}\) is injective.

  5. (iii)

    \(\mathfrak{d}\) is nilpotent and identical with its own normalizer.

We have the implications: \[ (\mathrm{i}) \Leftrightarrow (\mathrm{i\ bis}) \Rightarrow (\mathrm{ii}) \Leftrightarrow (\mathrm{ii\ bis}) \Leftrightarrow (\mathrm{iii}) \] (and we shall see in 5.7 a) that if \(\mathfrak{g}\) is the Lie algebra of a smooth algebraic group, then all the preceding conditions are equivalent).

The equivalence of (i) and (i bis) is trivial by 4.3, and these conditions trivially imply (ii). The equivalence of (ii) and (ii bis) is likewise trivial, as is (ii bis) \(\Rightarrow\) (iii) (cf. 4.1). It remains to prove the implication (iii)\(\Rightarrow\)(ii bis), the only one, moreover, which uses the fact that \(k\) is infinite, and which follows at once from the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.5

Let \(\mathfrak{d}\) be a nilpotent Lie algebra over an infinite field \(k\), acting on a finite-dimensional vector space V. Suppose that for every \(x \in \mathfrak{d}\), the endomorphism \(u(x)\) is not injective. Then there exists a non-zero element \(v\) of V annihilated by \(\mathfrak{d}\).

We may assume \(k\) algebraically closed and \(\mathfrak{d}\) of finite dimension. It is then known that V is the direct sum of a finite number of non-zero stable vector subspaces \(\mathrm{V}_i\) (\(1 \leqslant i \leqslant n\)), such that for every \(i\), and every \(x \in \mathfrak{d}\), \(u(x)|_{\mathrm{V}_i}\) has a single eigenvalue \(\lambda_i(x)\) (cf. Bourbaki, Groupes et Algèbres de Lie, Chap. I, § 4, Exercise 22). Let \(c_i(x)\) be the constant term of the characteristic polynomial of \(u(x)|_{\mathrm{V}_i}\), so that \(\lambda_i(x) = 0\) if and only if \(c_i(x) = 0\). Then \(c_i\) is a polynomial function on \(\mathfrak{d}\), and the hypothesis means that \(\mathfrak{d}\) is the union of the zero sets of the \(c_i\). Hence one of the \(c_i\) is zero, which brings us (replacing V by \(\mathrm{V}_i\)) to the case where V is such that the \(u(x)\) (\(x \in \mathfrak{d}\)) are nilpotent. But then Engel's theorem (Bourbaki loc. cit. th. 1) implies that there exists a non-zero \(v\) in V annihilated by \(\mathfrak{d}\). Q.E.D.

One sees easily that (\(k\) still being an infinite field) conditions (i), (i bis) of 4.4 are invariant under any extension of the base field. If they are fulfilled, we shall say that \(\mathfrak{d}\) is a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\); in the general case (\(k\) not necessarily infinite) we shall say that \(\mathfrak{d}\) is a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) if it becomes a Cartan subalgebra after one (and hence any) extension of the base field \(k \to k'\), with \(k'\) infinite. This therefore implies that \(\mathfrak{d}\) is nilpotent and equal to its own normalizer.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.6
  1. a)

    Let \(a\) be an element of \(\mathfrak{g}\). If \(a\) is regular, it is contained in one and only one Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) (and we shall see in 6.1 d) the converse when \(k\) is algebraically closed and \(\mathfrak{g}\) is the Lie algebra of a smooth algebraic group).

  2. b)

    Let \(\mathfrak{d}\) be a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\), and \(a\) an element of \(\mathfrak{d}\); then \(a\) is regular in \(\mathfrak{g}\) if and only if \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{d}}\) is injective.

Indeed, for a) one notes that if \(a\) is regular, then \(\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\) is a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) (for this is true over an infinite extension \(k'\) of \(k\)), and it then follows at once from 4.3 that every Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) containing \(a\) is identical with the preceding one. For b) one notes that the nullity9 of \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}}\) is equal to the sum of the nullities of \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{d}}\) and of \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{d}}\), and since the first equals \(r\), the sum is equal to \(r\) if and only if \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{d}}\) is injective. Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.7

Let \(a\) be a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}\), \(\mathfrak{d}\) a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) containing \(a\), A an algebra over \(k\), \(\mathfrak{g}_{\mathrm{A}}\) and \(\mathfrak{d}_{\mathrm{A}} \subset \mathfrak{g}_{\mathrm{A}}\) the A-Lie algebras deduced from \(\mathfrak{g}, \mathfrak{d}\) by base change, and \(a_{\mathrm{A}}\) the image of \(a\) in \(\mathfrak{g}_{\mathrm{A}}\). Let \(u\) be an automorphism of \(\mathfrak{g}_{\mathrm{A}}\). For \(u(\mathfrak{d}_{\mathrm{A}}) = \mathfrak{d}_{\mathrm{A}}\) to hold, it is necessary and sufficient that \(u(a_{\mathrm{A}}) \in \mathfrak{d}_{\mathrm{A}}\).

The condition is trivially necessary; let us prove that it is also sufficient. If it is fulfilled, then \(\mathfrak{d}' = u(\mathfrak{d}_{\mathrm{A}})\) is a Lie subalgebra containing \(a_{\mathrm{A}}\), every element \(b\) of which is such that \(\mathrm{ad}(b)_{\mathfrak{d}'}\) is nilpotent (for \(\mathfrak{d}'\) is isomorphic to \(\mathfrak{d}_{\mathrm{A}}\), which has this property, as follows at once from the definition of "nilpotent" in Bourbaki, Groupes et Algèbres de Lie, Chap. I, § 4, def. 1). Taking \(b = a_{\mathrm{A}}\), one sees that the nil-space \(\mathrm{Nil}(b,\mathfrak{g}_{\mathrm{A}})\) contains \(\mathfrak{d}'\); on the other hand it is equal to \(\mathfrak{d}_{\mathrm{A}}\), and since \(\mathfrak{d}'\) is locally a direct factor in the module \(\mathfrak{g}_{\mathrm{A}}\) (\(\mathfrak{d}\) being so), hence in \(\mathfrak{d}_{\mathrm{A}}\), and it is a projective module of the same rank \(r\) as the latter, one concludes that it is equal to it. Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.8

Let \(\mathfrak{h}\) be a Lie subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

  1. a)

    The following conditions are equivalent if \(k\) is infinite:

    1. (i)

      \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains a Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

    2. (ii)

      \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains a regular element \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\), and an element \(b\) such that \(\mathrm{ad}(b)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}}\) is injective.

    3. (iii)

      \(\mathfrak{h}\) has the same nilpotent rank as \(\mathfrak{g}\), and contains a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

    These conditions are invariant under extension of the base field \(k\).

  2. b)

    Suppose that these conditions are satisfied over a suitable infinite extension \(k'\) of \(k\). Let \(a \in \mathfrak{h}\); then \(a\) is regular in \(\mathfrak{g}\) if and only if it is regular in \(\mathfrak{h}\) and \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}}\) is injective, i.e. if and only if \(\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{h}) = \mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\) and this is a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{h}\).

  3. c)

    Under the conditions of b), let \(\mathfrak{d}\) be a Lie subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{h}\); for it to be a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{h}\), it suffices that it be a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) (and we shall see in 5.8 that the condition is also necessary if \(\mathfrak{g}\) is the Lie algebra of a smooth algebraic group G, and \(\mathfrak{h}\) the Lie algebra of a smooth algebraic subgroup H of G).

One sees at once that conditions (ii) and (iii) of a) are invariant under extension of the base field \(k\) (assumed infinite), and that in the statements b) and c) we may assume \(k\) infinite, which we shall do. If \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains the Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d} = \mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\), then \(a\) is a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}\) such that \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}}\) is injective, hence (i)\(\Rightarrow\) (ii). Conversely, if (ii) is satisfied, then for a "sufficiently general" element \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{h}\), \(a\) simultaneously satisfies the two conditions considered in (ii), so \(\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\) is a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) and is contained in \(\mathfrak{h}\), so we have (i). Hence (i) and (ii) are equivalent. Suppose they are satisfied, and let \(a\) be a variable element of \(\mathfrak{h}\); then \[ (x) \qquad \mathrm{rang}_k\,\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g}) = \mathrm{rang}_k\,\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{h}) + \mathrm{rang}_k\,\mathrm{Nil}(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}}), \] on the other hand, the two terms of the right-hand side are respectively \(\geqslant r' =\) the nilpotent rank of \(\mathfrak{h}\), and \(\geqslant 0\), the equalities being moreover attained10 for a "sufficiently general" element of \(\mathfrak{h}\). Moreover, we also have \(\mathrm{rang}_k\,\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g}) \geqslant r =\) the nilpotent rank of \(\mathfrak{g}\), equality being attained for a "sufficiently general" element of \(\mathfrak{h}\), and being attained if and only if \(a\) is regular in \(\mathfrak{g}\). One concludes from this that \(r = r'\), and that \(a\) is regular if and only if the two terms of the right-hand side of (x) are equal respectively to \(r'\) and to 0, i.e. if and only if \(a\) is regular in \(\mathfrak{h}\) and \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}}\) is injective, which proves b); and c) follows from it trivially by taking an element \(a\) in \(\mathfrak{d}\) regular in \(\mathfrak{g}\), so that \(\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g}) = \mathfrak{d}\). Furthermore, the preceding result shows that (i)\(\Rightarrow\)(iii); finally (iii)\(\Rightarrow\)(i), for, given (iii), a sufficiently general element \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{h}\) is regular in \(\mathfrak{h}\) and in \(\mathfrak{g}\), so \(\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{h}) \subset \mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\) are respectively Cartan subalgebras of \(\mathfrak{h}\) and of \(\mathfrak{g}\), and since they have the same rank over \(k\), they are identical, which proves (i). This completes the proof of 4.8.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: i.e., the dimension of the nil-space.
N.D.E.: we have replaced "the equality" by "the equalities".

pages.title: 5. Case of the Lie algebra of a smooth algebraic group: density theorem

blocks.html: 5. Case of the Lie algebra of a smooth algebraic group: density theorem

Let G be a smooth algebraic group over the field \(k\), and \(\mathfrak{g}\) its Lie algebra. Let \(\mathfrak{h}\) be a Lie subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\). Let G act on \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) by the adjoint representation, and consider the subscheme \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{h})\). The construction of Section 1 leads us to introduce \[ \mathrm{N} = \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathfrak{h}) = \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{h})), \] which is an algebraic subgroup of G (not necessarily smooth), \[ \mathfrak{n} = \mathrm{Lie}(\mathrm{N}), \] and the scheme \[ \mathrm{X} = \mathrm{G}\times^{\mathrm{N}}\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{h}) \] quotient of \(\mathrm{X} = \mathrm{G}\times\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{h})\) by N acting on the right by \((g,x)\cdot n = (gn, \mathrm{Ad}(n^{-1})x)\). We consider the canonical morphisms \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{G}\times \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{h}) \ar[d]_-{q} \ar[dr]^-{\varphi} & \\ \mathrm{X} \ar[r]_-{\psi} & \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}). } \]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.1

With the preceding notation, suppose \(k\) infinite. Consider the following conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains a Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

  2. (ii)

    There exists \(a \in \mathfrak{h}\) such that \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}}\) is injective.

  3. (iii)

    \(\varphi : \mathrm{G}\times\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{h}) \to \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) is generically smooth.

  4. (iv)

    The preceding morphism \(\varphi\) (or equivalently \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\)) is dominant, and \(\mathfrak{h}\) has the same nilpotent rank as \(\mathfrak{g}\).

  5. (v)

    \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) is generically smooth and \(\mathfrak{h} = \mathfrak{n}\).

  6. (vi)
