    \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) is dominant, and \(\mathfrak{h} = \mathfrak{n}\).

  7. (vii)

    \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) is dominant.

  8. (viii)

    \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) is dominant, and N is smooth.

  9. (ix)

    \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) is dominant, and \(\mathfrak{h} = \mathfrak{n}\), and \(\mathfrak{h}\) is the Lie algebra of a smooth algebraic subgroup H of G.

  10. (x)

    \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) is generically quasi-finite, and \(\mathfrak{n} = \mathfrak{h}\).

  11. (xi)

    \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) is generically étale, and \(\mathfrak{n} = \mathfrak{h}\).

  12. (xii)

    There exists a smooth algebraic subgroup H of G with Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{h}\), and \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains a Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

  13. (xiii)

    There exists \(a \in \mathfrak{h}\) such that N and the transporter \(\mathrm{M}_a\) of \(a\) into \(\mathfrak{h}\) (cf. Section 1) coincide in a neighborhood of \(e\), and \(\mathfrak{n} = \mathfrak{h}\).

We then have the following diagram of implications: \[ \xymatrix{ (\mathrm{xi}) \ar@{<=>}[r] & (\mathrm{xii}) \ar[r] \ar[dl] & (\mathrm{xiii}) \ar[r] & (\mathrm{viii}) \ar@{<=>}[r] & (\mathrm{ix}) \ar@{<=>}[r] \ar[dr] & (\mathrm{x}) & \\ (\mathrm{i}) \ar@{<=>}[r] & (\mathrm{ii}) \ar@{<=>}[r] & (\mathrm{iii}) \ar@{<=>}[r] & (\mathrm{iv}) \ar[r] & (\mathrm{v}) \ar[r] & (\mathrm{vi}) \ar[r] & (\mathrm{vii}) } \] When \(k\) is of characteristic zero, all the conditions considered are equivalent. Finally, we have \[ (\mathrm{xi}) \Leftrightarrow [(\mathrm{i}) \text{ and } (\mathrm{viii})] \Leftrightarrow [(\mathrm{v}) \text{ and } (\mathrm{viii})]. \]

Let us first note the trivial implications: \[ (\mathrm{v})\Rightarrow(\mathrm{vi})\Rightarrow(\mathrm{vii}) \ , \qquad (\mathrm{ix})\Rightarrow(\mathrm{vi}) \ , \qquad (\mathrm{xi})\Leftrightarrow[(\mathrm{x}) \text{ and } (\mathrm{v})]. \]

Let us prove the equivalence of conditions (i) to (iv) and the fact that they imply (v). The implication (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii) is trivial. On the other hand, (iii) means, when \(k\) is algebraically closed, that there exists a point of \(\mathrm{G}\times\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{h})\) rational over \(k\) at which the tangent map of \(\varphi\) is surjective, and one sees at once that this point may be taken of the form \((e,a)\), where \(a \in \mathfrak{h}\) (transforming it, if necessary, by an operation of \(\mathrm{G}(k)\)). One concludes that if \(k\) is infinite (not necessarily algebraically closed), this (obviously sufficient) condition for generic smoothness is still necessary. Now the tangent map is easily computed: identifying the tangent space to \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{h})\) at \(a\) with \(\mathfrak{h}\), it is the map \[ (\xi,x) \longmapsto [\xi,a] + x \] from \(\mathfrak{g}\times\mathfrak{h}\) to \(\mathfrak{g}\). To say that it is surjective also means that \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}}\) is surjective, or what amounts to the same thing, injective. This proves the equivalence of conditions (ii) and (iii). Moreover, (ii) obviously implies \(\mathfrak{h} = \mathfrak{n}\), and (iii) implies that \(\psi\) is generically smooth, for if \(\varphi\) is smooth at a point \(u\), it follows (\(q\) being flat) that \(\psi\) is smooth at \(q(u)\). Hence (ii), (iii) imply (v). Let us prove that they imply (i). To this end, note that since \(\psi\) is dominant, and the set of regular points of \(\mathfrak{g}\) is open and dense, it follows that \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains regular elements of \(\mathfrak{g}\), hence that a "sufficiently general" element \(b\) of \(\mathfrak{h}\) is regular in \(\mathfrak{g}\) and satisfies \(\mathrm{ad}(b)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}}\) injective, hence \(\mathrm{Nil}(b,\mathfrak{g}) \subset \mathfrak{h}\), so \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains the Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d} = \mathrm{Nil}(b,\mathfrak{g})\). Hence (i), (ii), (iii) are equivalent; finally (i) \(\Leftrightarrow\)(iv), for we have already remarked that if \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains a Cartan subalgebra, it has the same rank as \(\mathfrak{g}\) (4.6), so (i)\(\Rightarrow\)(iv); conversely, if (iv) is satisfied, then \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}\), and since it has the same rank as \(\mathfrak{g}\), it contains a Cartan subalgebra by virtue of 4.6.
Let us prove the equivalence of conditions (viii) to (x). Let us first remark on the following facts:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2
  1. a)

    If N is smooth, then \[ \dim \mathrm{X} \leqslant \dim \mathrm{G}, \] equality holding if and only if \(\mathfrak{h} = \mathfrak{n}\).

  2. b)

    If \(\mathfrak{h} = \mathfrak{n}\), then \[ \dim \mathrm{X} \geqslant \dim \mathrm{G}, \] equality holding if and only if N is smooth.

These assertions follow at once from the formula \(\dim \mathrm{X} = \dim \mathrm{G} - \dim \mathrm{N} + \dim_k \mathfrak{h}\), and from the fact that \(\dim \mathrm{N} = \dim_k \mathfrak{n}\) is equivalent to N smooth.

This being said, (viii)\(\Rightarrow\) (x), for (viii) implies \(\dim \mathrm{X} \geqslant \dim \mathrm{G}\), hence by virtue of 5.2 a) the equality of these dimensions and \(\mathfrak{h} = \mathfrak{n}\), hence (x); and one sees likewise (x) \(\Rightarrow\)(viii) by applying 5.2 b). On the other hand, (viii) implies (ix), for it implies \(\mathfrak{h} = \mathfrak{n}\), so \(\mathfrak{h}\) is the Lie algebra of the smooth algebraic subgroup N of G; and conversely (ix) \(\Rightarrow\) (viii), for since H normalizes its Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{h}\), it is contained in N, and since H is smooth and has the same Lie algebra as N, it follows that N is smooth.

Let us finally prove the equivalence of conditions (xi), (xii), (xiii) and the fact that they entail (iii) (which will complete the establishment of our diagram of implications). We have (xi) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (xiii), for if \(\mathfrak{n} = \mathfrak{h}\), then by virtue of 5.2 b) we have \(\dim \mathrm{X} \geqslant \dim \mathrm{G}\), so (xi) is then equivalent (taking into account that \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) is normal) to the fact that \(\psi\) is generically unramified, which is also equivalent to (xiii) thanks to (1.1 (ii) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (iii)), proceeding as above in the proof of (ii) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (iii). Since (xi) \(\Rightarrow\) (x) \(\Rightarrow\) (viii) by what we have already seen, one sees that (xi) implies that N is smooth, i.e. \(q : \mathrm{G}\times\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{h}) \to \mathrm{X}\) is smooth, so the composite \(\varphi = \psi\circ q\) is generically smooth, i.e. we have (iii). Since (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i), it also follows that (xi) \(\Rightarrow\) (xii)11. Finally (xii) \(\Rightarrow\) (xi), for we obviously have (xii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i), so, as we have seen (i)\(\Rightarrow\)(iii)\(\Rightarrow\)(v), we have (xii) \(\Rightarrow\) (v); it follows that we also have (xii) \(\Rightarrow\) (ix), and since we have seen (ix) \(\Rightarrow\) (x), it follows that (xii) \(\Rightarrow\) ((v) and (x)), hence (xii) \(\Rightarrow\) (xi), since generically étale = generically smooth + generically quasi-finite.

Finally, when \(k\) is of characteristic 0, then (vii) \(\Rightarrow\) (viii), for by virtue of a theorem of Cartier, N is automatically smooth (VIB 1.6.1), and [(viii) and (x)] \(\Rightarrow\) (xi), since in characteristic zero, for a morphism of integral preschemes, generically étale = dominant and generically quasi-finite. This shows that in this case, all the conditions (i) to (xiii) are equivalent.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.3

Under the equivalent conditions (viii) to (x), there exists a unique connected smooth algebraic subgroup H of G whose Lie algebra is \(\mathfrak{h}\), and we have \[ \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{H}) = \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathfrak{h}) = \mathrm{N}, \qquad \mathrm{H} = \mathrm{N}^0. \]

Indeed, \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{N}^0\) will satisfy the desired conditions; on the other hand, if H satisfies them, then (since H normalizes its Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{h}\)) we have \(\mathrm{H} \subset \mathrm{N}\), hence, since this is an inclusion of smooth groups having the same Lie algebra, with H connected, we shall have \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{N}^0\). For the identity \(\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{H}) = \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathfrak{h})\), we may assume \(k\) algebraically closed; then, from what we have just seen, it follows immediately that the points of the two groups with values in \(k\) are the same; on the other hand, the inclusions \(\mathrm{H} \subset \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{H}) \subset \mathrm{N}\) show that \(\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{H})\) and N have the same Lie algebra, hence they are identical.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.4

Under the equivalent conditions (i) to (iv), let \(a \in \mathfrak{h}\). Then the following conditions are equivalent, and are realized if \(a\) is regular in \(\mathfrak{g}\):

  1. (i)

    \(\varphi\) is smooth at \((e,a)\).

  2. (ii)

    \(\mathrm{M}_a = \mathrm{Transp}_{\mathrm{G}}(a,\mathfrak{h})\) is smooth at \(e\), and \(\dim_e(\mathrm{M}_a) = \mathrm{rang}_k\,\mathfrak{h}\).

  3. (iii)

    \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{h}}\) is injective (or equivalently, bijective).

When we are under the equivalent conditions (xi) to (xiii), let H be the algebraic subgroup of G considered in 5.3. Then the preceding conditions are also equivalent to the following conditions:

  1. (iv)

    \(\psi\) is étale at \((\overline{e},a)\).

  2. (v)

    Denoting by \(\mathrm{M}_a^0\) the connected component of \(e\) in the transporter \(\mathrm{M}_a\) of \(a\) into \(\mathfrak{h}\), equipped with the structure induced by \(\mathrm{M}_a\), we have \[ \mathrm{H} = \mathrm{M}_a^0. \]

Obviously (i)\(\Rightarrow\)(ii), since \(\mathrm{M}_a\) is isomorphic to the fiber \(\varphi^{-1}(a)\), the point \(e\) corresponding to \((e,a)\); and we have (ii)\(\Rightarrow\)(i), for (ii) implies that \(\varphi\) is "equidimensional" at \((e,a)\) (i.e. the dimension of the fiber passing through this point is that of the generic fiber), which implies (\(\mathrm{G}\times\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{h})\) and \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) being regular) that it is flat at \((e,a)\), hence smooth, since its fiber is smooth at this point. The equivalence of (i) and (iii) was seen in the proof of 5.1, as resulting from the simple computation of the tangent map. Moreover, we saw in 4.8 b) that "\(a\) regular in \(\mathfrak{g}\)" \(\Rightarrow\)(iii). Under conditions (xi) to (xiii), since \(q : \mathrm{G}\times\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{h}) \to \mathrm{X}\) is smooth (N being smooth), it follows that (i) is equivalent to \(\psi\) smooth at \((\overline{e},a)\), and since \(\psi\) is generically étale, this is equivalent to (iv). Finally, as was pointed out at the end of the proof of 1.1, (iv) implies that N is the prescheme induced on \(\mathrm{M}_a\) by an open and closed part of \(\mathrm{M}_a\), whence (v); finally (v)\(\Rightarrow\)(ii) trivially (or again (v)\(\Rightarrow\) (iv) by 1.1, for \(\psi\) being dominant and \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) normal, "unramified" is here equivalent to "étale"); this completes the proof of 5.4.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.5

Let G be a smooth algebraic group over a field \(k\), and H a smooth algebraic subgroup such that its Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains (at least over a suitable extension of \(k\)) a Cartan subalgebra of the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}\) of G. Let K be a connected algebraic subgroup of G (not necessarily smooth), with Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{k}\), and suppose that \(\mathfrak{k}\) contains a regular element \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\) (at least over a suitable extension of \(k\)). Then H contains K if and only if \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains \(\mathfrak{k}\).

Indeed, by virtue of 5.4 (iii)\(\Rightarrow\)(v) we have \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{M}_a^0\) (N.B. of course, this relation being invariant under extension of the base field, it is valid without the hypothesis that the latter be infinite!); on the other hand, \(\mathfrak{k} \subset \mathfrak{h}\) obviously implies \(\mathrm{K} \subset \mathrm{M}_a\), hence, K being connected, \(\mathrm{K} \subset \mathrm{M}_a^0\), whence \(\mathrm{K} \subset \mathrm{H}\). Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.6

Let G, H be as in 5.5 and let K be an algebraic subgroup of G; we suppose H connected and K smooth. Then K contains H if and only if \(\mathfrak{k}\) contains \(\mathfrak{h}\).

Indeed, if \(\mathfrak{k} \supset \mathfrak{h}\), then K satisfies the hypothesis considered in 5.5 for H; on the other hand, H obviously satisfies the condition considered for K in 5.5. The conclusion then follows from 5.5.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.7

Let \(\mathfrak{g}\) be the Lie algebra of a smooth algebraic group G over a field \(k\). Then:

  1. a)

    Let \(\mathfrak{d}\) be a Lie subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\). For \(\mathfrak{d}\) to be a Cartan subalgebra, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathfrak{d}\) be nilpotent and equal to its own normalizer.

  2. b)

    Let \(a\) be an element of \(\mathfrak{g}\); for \(a\) to be regular, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\) be nilpotent.

After an extension of the base field if necessary, we may assume \(k\) infinite. Taking 4.4 into account, we are reduced, for a), to proving that if \(\mathfrak{d}\) is nilpotent and contains an element \(a\) such that \(\mathrm{ad}(a)_{\mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{d}}\) is injective, then \(\mathfrak{d}\) is a Cartan subalgebra. Now, by virtue of 5.1 (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i), \(\mathfrak{d}\) contains a Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}' = \mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\), and by virtue of 4.3 one concludes from the fact that \(\mathfrak{d}\) is nilpotent that \(\mathfrak{d} = \mathfrak{d}'\). To prove b), one notes that \(\mathrm{Nil}(a,\mathfrak{g})\) is a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) thanks to a), so \(a\) is regular.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.8

Let G be a smooth algebraic group over a field \(k\), H a smooth algebraic subgroup, \(\mathfrak{g}, \mathfrak{h}\) the Lie algebras, and suppose that, after a suitable extension of the base field, \(\mathfrak{h}\) contains a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\). Let \(\mathfrak{d}\) be a subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{h}\); then it is a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{h}\) if and only if it is a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

Taking 4.8 c) into account, it remains to show that if \(\mathfrak{d}\) is a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{h}\), it is a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\), and for this we are reduced to showing that \(\mathfrak{d}\) contains an element \(a\) regular in \(\mathfrak{g}\), assuming (as is permissible) \(k\) algebraically closed. But since there is a dense open subset of \(\mathfrak{h}\) consisting of regular points of \(\mathfrak{g}\), our assertion follows from 5.1 (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (vii) applied to \((\mathfrak{h},\mathfrak{d})\).

pages.title: 6. Cartan subalgebras and subgroups of type (C), relative to a smooth algebraic group

blocks.html: 6. Cartan subalgebras and subgroups of type (C), relative to a smooth algebraic group
For simplicity, we restrict ourselves in the following theorem to the case of an algebraically closed base field (the case of an arbitrary base prescheme being treated in the next exposé):

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.1

Let G be a smooth algebraic group over an algebraically closed field \(k\), and \(\mathfrak{g}\) its Lie algebra. Then:

  1. a)

    The Cartan subalgebras of \(\mathfrak{g}\) are conjugate.

  2. b)

    Let \(\mathfrak{d}\) be a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\). Then its normalizer N in G is smooth, and \(\mathrm{D} = \mathrm{N}^0\) is the only connected smooth subgroup of G whose Lie algebra is \(\mathfrak{d}\). We have \[ \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathfrak{d}) = \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{D}) = \mathrm{N}, \qquad \text{hence} \quad \mathrm{D} = \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{D})^0. \]

  3. c)

    With \(\mathfrak{d}\) as in b), set, as in Section 5: \(\mathrm{X} = \mathrm{G}\times^{\mathrm{N}}\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{d})\), and consider the canonical morphism \[ \psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g}), \] (whose fiber at \(a \in \mathfrak{g}\) has as points with values in \(k\) the set of Cartan subalgebras of \(\mathfrak{g}\) containing \(a\)). Then \(\psi\) is a birational morphism.

  4. d)

    With the notation of c), let U be the largest open subset of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) such that \(\psi\) induces an isomorphism \[ \psi^{-1}(\mathrm{U}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{U}. \] Then for \(a \in \mathfrak{g}\), the following conditions are equivalent:

    1. (i)

      \(a \in \mathrm{U}(k)\).

    2. (ii)

      \(a\) is contained in one and only one Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

    3. (iii)

      The set of Cartan subalgebras of \(\mathfrak{g}\) containing \(a\) is finite and non-empty.

    4. (iv)

      The fiber \(\psi^{-1}(a)\) has an isolated point.

    5. (v)

      (If \(a \in \mathfrak{d}\)) The morphism \(\psi\) is étale (or merely: quasi-finite) at the point \((\overline{e},a)\).

    6. (vi)

      \(a\) is a regular element of \(\mathfrak{g}\).

    7. (vii)

      With the notation of 5.4 (iii), we have \(\mathrm{N} = \mathrm{M}_a\).

Proof. Let us apply 5.1 when \(\mathfrak{h}\) is a Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\), and show that the strongest conditions (xi) to (xiii) are then satisfied. This is evident, either in the form (xiii), taking 4.7 into account (which implies that for a regular element \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\) contained in \(\mathfrak{d}\), we have \(\mathrm{N} = \mathrm{M}_a\)), or in the form (xi)=(x)+(i), for condition (i) is trivial and condition (x) follows from the fact that a regular point of \(\mathfrak{g}\) is contained in a single Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\) (4.6 a)), and a fortiori in a single conjugate of \(\mathfrak{d}\). Then b) follows from 5.3, and c) follows from the fact that \(\psi\) is generically étale and that a sufficiently general point (more precisely, a regular point) of \(\mathfrak{g}\) is contained in a single Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\). Under these conditions, the equivalence of conditions (i) to (v) on \(a\) is an immediate consequence of Zariski's Main Theorem for the separated birational morphism \(\psi\), taking into account that \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) is normal and X is integral. The equivalence of (v) and (vi) is a special case of 5.4 (iii)\(\Leftrightarrow\)(iv) (reducing to the case where \(a \in \mathfrak{d}\) by transforming \(a\) by a suitable element \(g \in \mathrm{G}(k)\)), taking 4.6 b) into account. Moreover, by 5.4, (v) and (vi) are also equivalent to \(\mathrm{N} \supset \mathrm{M}_a^0\), and by virtue of 4.7, already invoked, this even implies \(\mathrm{N} = \mathrm{M}\). This proves d). Of course, b), c), and the equivalence of (i), (iv), (v), (vi), (vii) remain valid over an arbitrary base field.

Let us now prove a), using the fact that \(k\) is algebraically closed. By virtue of 5.1 (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (vii), \(\psi : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) is dominant, so there exists a dense open subset V of \(\mathrm{W}(\mathfrak{g})\) such that every \(a \in \mathrm{V}(k)\) is the image of an element of \(\mathrm{X}(k)\), i.e. is contained in a conjugate of \(\mathfrak{d}\). Applying this result to a second Cartan subalgebra \(\mathfrak{d}'\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\), one sees that V may be taken such that every element of \(\mathrm{V}(k)\) is conjugate to an element of \(\mathfrak{d}\) and to an element of \(\mathfrak{d}'\). Taking a regular element in \(\mathrm{V}(k)\), it follows that there is a conjugate \(\mathfrak{d}''\) of \(\mathfrak{d}'\) containing an element of \(\mathfrak{d}\) regular in \(\mathfrak{g}\), hence equal to \(\mathfrak{d}\) by virtue of 4.6 a). This completes the proof of 6.1.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.2

Let G be a smooth algebraic group over a field \(k\). We call a subgroup of type (C) of G any connected smooth algebraic subgroup whose Lie algebra is a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\). We call the infinitesimal rank of G the nilpotent rank of its Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}\), equal to the dimension of any subgroup of type (C) of G.
From 6.1 b) one concludes at once:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.3

Under the conditions of 6.2, the map \(\mathrm{D} \mapsto \mathfrak{d} = \mathrm{Lie}\,\mathrm{D}\) establishes a one-to-one correspondence between subgroups of type (C) of G and Cartan subalgebras \(\mathfrak{d}\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\). If D is a subgroup of type (C) of G, D is its own connected normalizer: \[ \mathrm{D} = \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{D})^0. \]
Combining 6.1 (a) and 6.3, one finds:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.4

If \(k\) is algebraically closed, the subgroups of type (C) of G are conjugate to one another.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.5

Let G be a smooth algebraic group over an algebraically closed field \(k\), H a smooth algebraic subgroup of G, and \(\mathfrak{g}\) and \(\mathfrak{h}\) the Lie algebras. For \(\mathfrak{h}\) to contain a Cartan subalgebra of \(\mathfrak{g}\), it is necessary and sufficient that H contain a subgroup D of type (C) of G.

It is obviously sufficient, and it is also necessary, for, in order that \(\mathrm{H} \supset \mathrm{D}\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathfrak{h} \supset \mathfrak{d}\), by virtue of 5.5.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 6.6
  1. a)

    Note that the connected subgroups H of G described in 6.5 correspond one-to-one to the Lie subalgebras \(\mathfrak{h}\) of \(\mathfrak{g}\) that satisfy the strongest condition (xii) of 5.1 (and by virtue of 5.5, the inclusion relations between such subgroups can already be read off from their Lie algebras).

  2. b)

    Suppose still that \(k\) is algebraically closed, and let D be a subgroup of type (C) of G; then it is easy to show that D contains a Cartan subgroup C of G: indeed, let \(\mathrm{V} = \mathfrak{g}/\mathfrak{d}\); then for a "general" element \(a\) of \(\mathfrak{d}\), \(\mathrm{ad}(a)\) acting on V is injective (it suffices that \(a\) be regular in \(\mathfrak{g}\)), from which one easily concludes that for \(g \in \mathrm{D}(k)\) "general", \(\mathrm{Ad}(g)\) acting on V has no non-zero invariant, which allows us to apply 2.1 (vii)\(\Rightarrow\) (i). In fact, we shall see in the next exposé a more precise result: every Cartan subgroup C of G is contained in one and only one subgroup of type (C) of G.

  3. c)

    One should beware of confusing, in general, Cartan subgroups and subgroups of type (C): the subgroups of type (C) of G are Cartan subgroups if and only if they are nilpotent (Cartan subgroups being indeed maximal nilpotent subgroups); now it can happen that \(\mathfrak{g}\) is nilpotent without G being so (example: \(\mathrm{SL}(2)_k\) for \(k\) of characteristic 2); then the Cartan subalgebras of \(\mathfrak{g}\) are identical with \(\mathfrak{g}\), i.e. G is a subgroup of type (C) of itself if G is connected, but it is not a Cartan subgroup of G! On the other hand, we shall see in XIV that if G is an adjoint semisimple group, then its subgroups of type (C) are its Cartan subgroups. Likewise, in characteristic 0, without restriction on the smooth algebraic group G over \(k\), the same conclusion holds: this follows at once from the fact that in characteristic 0, a connected algebraic group is nilpotent if (and only if) its Lie algebra is. This in turn follows at once from the fact that in characteristic zero, the center Z of the connected algebraic group G has as Lie algebra the center of \(\mathfrak{g}\) (for one obtains a priori the space of invariants of \(\mathfrak{g}\) under the adjoint representation of G; now, G being connected and \(k\) of characteristic zero, these are also the "invariants" in the infinitesimal sense of the adjoint representation of \(\mathfrak{g}\), i.e. the center of \(\mathfrak{g}\)), and that by Cartier's theorem (cf. VIB 1.6.1) Z is smooth.
